title: Epic: PDS-level branding — atproto-style theming via env vars
description:
## Goal
Bring ePDS auth-service theming to parity with atproto's oauth-provider customization system. A PDS operator should be able to set PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR (and optionally PDS_SERVICE_NAME, PDS_LOGO_URL, PDS_SIGN_IN_TEXT, PDS_TERMS_OF_SERVICE_URL, PDS_PRIVACY_POLICY_URL) and have ALL auth-service pages consistently themed.

## Context
- Currently the login page hardcodes #1A130F as brand color and #F2EBE4 as background. Recovery uses #0f1828. Consent/account pages use yet another set of hardcoded colors. There are 6 templates with 3 different CSS systems.
- atproto's pattern: PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR → parse hex → auto-compute contrast (WCAG luminance) + hue (for HSL palette) → inject :root CSS vars → all pages themed.
- PR #9 (css-injection-trusted-clients) adds per-client CSS overrides for trusted clients. This epic adds the PDS-operator-level defaults that sit underneath client overrides in the cascade.
- The ui-design branch had AUTH_BRAND_COLOR etc. but was never merged and used a simpler approach. This epic replaces that with the atproto pattern using PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR.

## Key Constraints
- Use PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR (not AUTH_BRAND_COLOR) since pds-core already has this env var available from the atproto fork.
- Auto-compute contrast color (black or white) via WCAG relative luminance. Auto-extract hue for HSL palette generation.
- All 6 auth-service page templates must use a single shared CSS module.
- Client CSS from PR #9 must cascade AFTER PDS branding (client overrides operator).
- No breaking changes to existing env vars.

## Success
- Set PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR=#2cce8a → all auth pages (login, recovery, consent, account-login, account-settings, error pages) use green primary with auto-computed dark contrast text.
- Unset PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR → pages use sensible defaults (current #1A130F).
- Trusted client branding.css still overrides PDS defaults.

title: Add color utility functions to shared package (parseHexColor, pickContrastColor, extractHue)
Verify UI doesn't claim OTP sent before it is (both flows)
description: In Flow 1, the page initially renders with subtitle 'Sending code to xx***@...' but the OTP hasn't been sent yet — it fires as a background client-side POST after page load. In Flow 2, after email form submit the button says 'Sending...' but we only transition to OTP step after a successful response. Audit both flows and ensure the UI never claims to have sent the OTP until the sendOtp() response confirms success. Fix if needed.
close_reason: Flow 1: server-rendered subtitle changed from 'Sending code to...' to empty string; JS updates it to 'We sent...' only after sendOtp() resolves successfully. Flow 2 was already correct.

title: Fix: recovery OTP form Verify button not in form-actions row — inconsistent with recovery form layout (from ePDS-aq2.5)
description:
Review of ePDS-aq2.5 found: The fac3f8d commit added a .form-actions row to renderRecoveryForm() (wrapping the Back link + Submit button in a space-between flex row), but renderOtpForm() was not updated consistently.

In renderOtpForm():
- Line 312: <button type="submit" class="btn-primary">Verify</button> is a bare button directly inside the <form>, with no .form-actions wrapper
- Lines 314-322: The .otp-links div (Resend Code + Back to sign in) is outside the form, below it

This means the OTP page has a different layout from the recovery form page:
- Recovery form: [Back to sign in] [Send Recovery Code] — space-between row via .form-actions
- OTP form: [Verify] (full-width? no — width:auto but no row), then [Resend Code] [Back to sign in] below

The .btn-primary has width:auto (set in fac3f8d), so the Verify button will be auto-sized but left-aligned with no counterpart on the left. The spec (ePDS-aq2.5) shows the OTP page should have the Resend/Back links below the Verify button, which is the current structure — but the Verify button should be right-aligned or the layout should be consistent.

Fix: Either wrap the Verify button in a .form-actions div (with the Resend/Back links as the left side), or document that the OTP page intentionally uses a different layout. If the intent is to match the recovery form's form-actions pattern, the otp-links should move inside a .form-actions wrapper alongside the Verify button.

title: Fix: isNewUser incorrectly true when PDS is unreachable in sendVerificationOTP (from ePDS-01p.1)
description: Review of ePDS-01p.1 found: In better-auth.ts line 195, the isNewUser flag is computed as '!result?.did'. With the new return type, result is null when the PDS is unreachable (network error / timeout). In that case, result?.did is undefined, so !result?.did is true — meaning isNewUser is incorrectly set to true when the PDS is down. This causes returning users to receive a 'Welcome' OTP email instead of a 'Sign in' email during PDS outages. The fix should distinguish the three cases: (1) result === null → PDS error, treat as unknown/existing (isNewUser = false, or skip the isNewUser determination entirely and fall back to a neutral template); (2) result.did === null → genuinely new user (isNewUser = true); (3) result.did is a string → existing user (isNewUser = false). Evidence: better-auth.ts line 195: const isNewUser = !result?.did — when result is null (PDS unreachable), this evaluates to true.

title: Fix: 'active sessions are listed' selector matches revoke-all form, not individual session rows (from ePDS-geiz)
description:
Review of ePDS-geiz found: account.steps.ts line 274 uses:
  const sessionRows = this.page.locator('form[action*="revoke"]')

The account settings page has two forms whose action contains 'revoke':
  1. /account/session/revoke (individual session revoke — only present when there are other sessions)
  2. /account/sessions/revoke-all (always present)

The 'active sessions are listed' step asserts sessionRows.first() is visible. This will always pass because the revoke-all form is always rendered, even when there are no other sessions to revoke. The step does not actually verify that individual session rows are listed.

Additionally, the 'the user revokes another session' step (line 280) filters buttons with hasText: /revoke/i, which matches both 'Revoke' (individual) and 'Revoke all sessions' (revoke-all). If only the current session exists, there are no individual Revoke buttons, but the 'Revoke all sessions' button would match the filter and be clicked, which is not the intended behavior.

Fix: use a more specific selector for individual session rows, e.g.:
  form[action='/account/session/revoke']
And for the revoke button filter, use exact text match:
  hasText: 'Revoke'  (not /revoke/i)
close_reason: 051234a fix: use exact selectors for individual session rows and revoke buttons

title: Fix: unnecessary null-coalescing on non-nullable 'did' in renderSettingsPage call (from ePDS-01p.3)
description: Review of ePDS-01p.3 found: In account-settings.ts line 118, the call 'did: did ?? "(unknown)"' uses the null-coalescing operator on 'did', which is typed as 'string' (non-nullable) after being cast from res.locals.did. This causes a @typescript-eslint/no-unnecessary-condition lint error. Evidence: 'pnpm lint' reports: account-settings.ts:118:14 error Unnecessary conditional, expected left-hand side of ?? operator to be possibly null or undefined. Before the refactor, 'did' was 'string | null' (the old getDidByEmail return type), so the ?? was valid. Now that the middleware guarantees did is a non-null string before next() is called, the fallback is dead code. Fix: change 'did: did ?? "(unknown)"' to 'did: did' on line 118.

title: Epic: E2E step definitions — 7 feature files (consent, account-settings, oauth-metadata, email-delivery, login-hint, auto-account-creation, pds-behavior-at-risk)
description:
## Goal
Wire up E2E step definitions for 7 of the 15 feature files, expanding coverage from 1 feature (passwordless-authentication) to 8 features (~53% of all scenarios). All tests run against live Railway services via `pnpm test:e2e`.

## Context
- E2E infrastructure already exists: cucumber-js + Playwright + Mailpit, running against Railway (ePDS-nr36 complete)
- Only `passwordless-authentication.feature` (5 scenarios) currently has step definitions
- 14 other feature files have Gherkin specs but no step definitions and are excluded from `cucumber.mjs paths`
- Mailpit is configured on Railway for OTP email retrieval
- Demo welcome page shows DID and handle (no access token in DOM)
- Demo callback converts all OAuth errors to `/?error=auth_failed`

## Features in scope
1. `oauth-metadata-override.feature` (2 of 3 scenarios) — pure HTTP fetch
2. `email-delivery.feature` (3 scenarios) — Mailpit assertions
3. `consent-screen.feature` (4 of 5 scenarios) — browser flow, reuses returning-user pattern
4. `login-hint-resolution.feature` (3-4 of 5 scenarios) — browser + demo login API
5. `account-settings.feature` (5-6 scenarios) — separate auth login flow at /account/login
6. `automatic-account-creation.feature` (2-3 of 4 scenarios) — XRPC assertions from welcome page
7. `pds-behavior-at-risk.feature` (2-3 of 8 scenarios) — HTTP-only PDS regression checks

## Key decisions
- Token-dependent scenarios (repo CRUD, getSession with bearer) are tagged `@pending` — no Playwright network interception for now
- Consent deny assertion checks for `auth_failed` text (demo swallows `access_denied`)
- Login hint Background creates account, captures real handle+DID from welcome page (ignores literal Gherkin strings)
- Mailpit helper extended to fetch message body (not just subject)
- `consent-screen.feature` Scenario 5 (CSS branding) tagged `@pending` — feature branch not merged
- `login-hint-resolution.feature` Scenario 4 (PAR body hint) tagged `@pending` — needs custom PAR client

## Out of scope
- security.feature (rate limiting, CSRF — intentionally deferred)
- tls-certificate-management.feature (Docker-only)
- client-branding.feature (feature branch not merged)
- social-login.feature (stays @manual)
- account-recovery.feature (complex orchestration, next batch)
- epds-callback.feature (HMAC tamper scenarios, lower value)
- Bearer token extraction via Playwright network interception

title: Extend EpdsWorld with userDid/userHandle fields and add createAccountViaOAuth helper to common.steps.ts
description:
## Files
- e2e/support/world.ts (modify)
- e2e/step-definitions/common.steps.ts (modify)
- e2e/support/mailpit.ts (modify)

## What to do

### 1. Add fields to EpdsWorld (world.ts)
Add these optional fields to the EpdsWorld class:
```ts
/** DID captured from the demo welcome page after successful login */
userDid?: string
/** Handle captured from the demo welcome page after successful login */
userHandle?: string
/** Body text of the most recent Mailpit email */
lastEmailBody?: string
```

### 2. Add getMessageBody to mailpit.ts
Add a function to fetch the full message body from Mailpit:
```ts
export async function getMessageBody(messageId: string): Promise<string> {
  const headers = { Authorization: mailpitAuthHeader() }
  const res = await fetch(`${testEnv.mailpitUrl}/api/v1/message/${messageId}`, { headers })
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`Mailpit GET message failed: ${res.status}`)
  const data = await res.json()
  // Mailpit returns { Text: "...", HTML: "..." } — prefer Text, fall back to HTML
  return data.Text || data.HTML || ""
}
```

### 3. Add createAccountViaOAuth helper to common.steps.ts
Extract the account creation logic from the existing `Given a returning user has a PDS account` step into a reusable async function. This helper:
1. Navigates to demoUrl
2. Fills #email with the provided email, submits
3. Waits for #step-otp.active (30s timeout)
4. Fetches OTP from Mailpit via waitForEmail + extractOtp
5. Fills #code with OTP, clicks #form-verify-otp .btn-primary
6. Waits for **/welcome URL (30s timeout)
7. Captures DID from page text (regex `/did:[a-z0-9:]+/i` on body text)
8. Captures handle from page text (the text after "Handle" label, starts with @)
9. Stores testEmail, userDid, userHandle on the world
10. Clears Mailpit inbox
11. Returns { did, handle }

```ts
export async function createAccountViaOAuth(
  world: EpdsWorld,
  email: string,
): Promise<{ did: string; handle: string }> {
  // ... implementation
}
```

### 4. Refactor existing returning-user step
The existing `Given a returning user has a PDS account` step should call createAccountViaOAuth internally, then reset the browser context (as it already does).

### 5. Add new Background steps used by other features
Register these step definitions:
- `Given {string} has an existing PDS account` — calls createAccountViaOAuth with a unique email (ignores the literal Gherkin email, stores on world). Returns pending if no Mailpit.
- `Given no PDS account exists for {string}` — no-op (each scenario uses unique emails, so no account exists). Store a unique email on this.testEmail.
- `Given a user account has been auto-created via the ePDS OAuth flow` — calls createAccountViaOAuth. Returns pending if no Mailpit.
- `Given {string} has a PDS account with handle {string}` — calls createAccountViaOAuth (ignores literal handle string, captures real handle). Returns pending if no Mailpit.

## Dont
- Do NOT use arrow functions for step definitions
- Do NOT import from monorepo packages (@certified-app/*)
- Do NOT try to set a specific handle — handles are randomly generated
- Do NOT store accessToken — that is deferred (marked @pending elsewhere)
close_reason: 321a889 feat: extend EpdsWorld, add getMessageBody, add createAccountViaOAuth helper

title: Create api.steps.ts — oauth-metadata-override + pds-behavior-at-risk HTTP-only scenarios
description:
## Files
- e2e/step-definitions/api.steps.ts (create)

## What to do
Create a new step definitions file for pure HTTP scenarios that need no browser — just fetch() calls against the PDS.

### Steps for oauth-metadata-override.feature

1. `When GET \/.well-known\/oauth-authorization-server is fetched from the PDS`
   - `fetch(testEnv.pdsUrl + "/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server")`
   - Store response JSON and headers on world (add `lastApiResponse?: { json: any; headers: Headers; status: number }` to EpdsWorld in this file — or use a local variable pattern with world)
   - Actually: store on world as `this.lastApiResponse`

2. `Then the response JSON includes authorization_endpoint pointing to the auth service`
   - Assert `this.lastApiResponse.json.authorization_endpoint` starts with `testEnv.authUrl`

3. `Then all other standard OAuth metadata fields are present (issuer, token_endpoint, etc.)`
   - Assert `issuer`, `token_endpoint`, `pushed_authorization_request_endpoint` are non-empty strings in the JSON

4. `Then the response has Cache-Control: public, max-age=300`
   - Assert `this.lastApiResponse.headers.get("cache-control")` contains `max-age=300`

5. `When GET \/.well-known\/oauth-authorization-server is fetched`
   - Same as step 1 (reuse or alias)

6. `Then the response includes token_endpoint pointing to the PDS's \/oauth\/token`
   - Assert `json.token_endpoint` contains `/oauth/token`

7. `Then the response includes pushed_authorization_request_endpoint pointing to \/oauth\/par`
   - Assert `json.pushed_authorization_request_endpoint` contains `/oauth/par`

8. `Then response_types_supported, grant_types_supported, and dpop_signing_alg_values_supported are present`
   - Assert all three are arrays with length > 0

### Steps for pds-behavior-at-risk.feature (HTTP-only subset)

9. `When a client calls GET \/xrpc\/com.atproto.server.describeServer`
   - `fetch(testEnv.pdsUrl + "/xrpc/com.atproto.server.describeServer")`
   - Store response JSON

10. `Then the response includes availableUserDomains and links`
    - Assert `json.availableUserDomains` is an array
    - Assert `json.links` is an object (or present)

11. `When someone calls com.atproto.server.createSession with a guessed password`
    - `fetch(testEnv.pdsUrl + "/xrpc/com.atproto.server.createSession", { method: "POST", headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }, body: JSON.stringify({ identifier: this.testEmail || "test@example.com", password: "wrongpassword123" }) })`
    - Store response

12. `Then the request fails with an authentication error`
    - Assert response status is 401 or 400
    - Assert response JSON contains error field

### Scenario 3 of oauth-metadata-override (full browser flow)
13. `Given a standard AT Protocol OAuth client` — return "pending" (needs custom PAR client)
14. All subsequent steps for that scenario — return "pending"

### World state
Add to EpdsWorld (or use a module-level Map keyed by world instance):
```ts
lastApiResponse?: { json: any; headers: Headers; status: number }
```
Since modifying world.ts is done in task .1, you can either:
- Add lastApiResponse to world.ts in this task too (its a simple addition)
- Or store it as a local variable in the step file using a WeakMap pattern

Prefer adding it to world.ts directly — simpler.

## Dont
- Do NOT use Playwright browser for these steps — pure fetch() only
- Do NOT import from monorepo packages
- Do NOT try to send a real PAR request (needs DPoP key generation) — mark that scenario @pending
- Do NOT hardcode URLs — use testEnv.pdsUrl and testEnv.authUrl

title: Extend auth.steps.ts — consent-screen.feature step definitions
description:
## Files
- e2e/step-definitions/auth.steps.ts (modify)

## What to do
Add step definitions for consent-screen.feature scenarios 1-4. Scenario 5 (CSS branding) is tagged @pending in the cucumber.mjs tags or in the feature file.

### Key context
- The consent page is at /auth/consent with buttons: `.btn-approve` (value="approve") and `.btn-deny` (value="deny")
- The consent page title is "Authorize Application" for existing users
- The consent page shows client name in `.subtitle` element: "<clientName> wants to access your account"
- Permissions are listed in `.permissions ul li` elements
- New users SKIP consent entirely — complete.ts line 103 checks `isNewAccount` and redirects to handle picker or random handle, never hitting /auth/consent
- Existing users see consent on FIRST login to a client — complete.ts line 100-101 checks `needsConsent = !isNewAccount && clientId && !ctx.db.hasClientLogin(email, clientId)`
- After approving, `recordClientLogin(email, clientId)` is called — subsequent logins skip consent
- Deny redirects to PDS with `error=access_denied`, which the demo callback converts to `/?error=auth_failed`
- Demo error page shows "Authentication failed. Please try again." text

### Steps to implement

1. `Given {string} has never logged into the demo client`
   - No-op. Each scenario uses a freshly created account (from the Background step "{string} has an existing PDS account" in task .1), so consent is always first-time.

2. `When {string} authenticates via OTP through the demo client`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Navigate to demoUrl, fill #email with this.testEmail (NOT the literal Gherkin email), submit
   - Wait for #step-otp.active (30s)
   - Fetch OTP from Mailpit, fill #code, click verify
   - Do NOT wait for /welcome — the flow may stop at /auth/consent

3. `Then a consent screen is displayed`
   - Wait for URL containing `/auth/consent` (timeout 30s)
   - Assert `.btn-approve` is visible

4. `And it shows the demo client's name and requested permissions`
   - Assert `.subtitle` text is not empty (contains client name)
   - Assert `.permissions li` has at least 1 element

5. `When the user clicks "Approve"`
   - Click `.btn-approve`

6. `When the user clicks "Deny"`
   - Click `.btn-deny`

7. `When {string} authenticates and reaches the consent screen`
   - Same as step 2 + step 3 combined (authenticate then assert consent visible)

8. `Then the demo client receives an "access_denied" error`
   - Wait for URL containing `error=auth_failed` (the demo swallows access_denied → auth_failed)
   - OR assert page body contains "Authentication failed"

9. `Given {string} has previously approved the demo client`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Call createAccountViaOAuth (from task .1) — this drives the FULL flow including consent approval
   - The createAccountViaOAuth helper goes through: login → consent (approve) → welcome page
   - IMPORTANT: After createAccountViaOAuth, the consent has been recorded. Reset browser context for the next step.
   - Actually: createAccountViaOAuth already handles the full flow. But it may not click Approve if consent is shown. Need to check: does the helper handle the consent screen?
   - RESOLUTION: createAccountViaOAuth waits for **/welcome. If consent appears in between, it needs to click Approve. Either:
     a) Modify createAccountViaOAuth to handle consent (click Approve if consent page appears), OR
     b) This step does its own full flow: navigate demo → email → OTP → consent appears → click Approve → welcome → reset context
   - Go with option (b) for clarity — this step does the full flow explicitly, including clicking Approve.

10. `When {string} authenticates again via the demo client`
    - Return pending if no Mailpit
    - Navigate to demoUrl, fill #email with this.testEmail, submit
    - Fetch OTP from Mailpit, fill #code, click verify
    - (consent should be skipped this time)

11. `Then no consent screen is shown`
    - Wait for URL matching **/welcome (30s) — if we get to welcome without hitting /auth/consent, consent was skipped
    - This is a negative assertion. The simplest approach: just wait for /welcome. If consent appeared, the flow would have stopped there and the /welcome wait would timeout.

12. `Then the browser is redirected directly to the demo client with a valid session`
    - Assert URL contains /welcome
    - Assert body contains "did:"

13. `Then no consent screen is shown (account creation implies consent)`
    - Same as step 11

14. `Then a PDS account is created`
    - Assert body contains "did:" on the welcome page

15. `Then the browser is redirected to the demo client with a valid session`
    - Same as step 12

16. `When {string} authenticates via OTP through the demo client` (for new user scenario)
    - Same as step 2 but with a unique email (this.testEmail set by "no PDS account exists for" Background step)

### IMPORTANT: consent screen handling in createAccountViaOAuth
The createAccountViaOAuth helper (task .1) navigates to /welcome after login. For NEW users, consent is skipped (complete.ts sends them to handle picker or random handle → epds-callback → /welcome). For the consent-screen tests, we need EXISTING users who have NOT yet approved the demo client. This means:
- "has an existing PDS account" Background step creates the account (first login, consent approved)
- But then the consent is already recorded! Subsequent logins skip consent.
- PROBLEM: We need an existing user who has NOT approved the demo client.

RESOLUTION: The createAccountViaOAuth helper creates the account AND approves consent (it goes through the full flow). So "{string} has an existing PDS account" creates an account that has already approved the demo client. For the consent test, we need a different approach:
- Create the account via a DIFFERENT client (not the demo client), OR
- Create the account and then clear the client_logins record, OR
- The simplest: for consent scenarios, the Background step should create the account but NOT go through the demo client. Since there is no other client, we need to handle this differently.

ACTUAL RESOLUTION: Look at complete.ts line 103-104. For NEW accounts with handleMode=random (or picker), the flow goes: login → handle picker/random → epds-callback → demo /welcome. The consent check is ONLY for existing users (isNewAccount=false). So when createAccountViaOAuth creates a NEW account, consent is NOT checked — the account is created and the flow completes. The client_login is NOT recorded for new accounts (line 183 only runs for existing users path 5d).

Wait — line 150 (random mode path 5a) does NOT call recordClientLogin. Line 163 (picker path 5b) redirects to choose-handle which eventually calls epds-callback. Let me check if epds-callback records client login... Looking at consent.ts line 150-152: `if (clientId) { ctx.db.recordClientLogin(email, clientId) }` — this is only in the consent POST handler.

So: NEW accounts created via createAccountViaOAuth do NOT get a client_login record. Their SECOND login via the demo client WILL show consent. This is exactly what we need!

So the flow for consent scenarios is:
1. Background: "{string} has an existing PDS account" → createAccountViaOAuth creates account (first login, no consent recorded)
2. Scenario step: "{string} authenticates via OTP through the demo client" → second login → consent screen appears

This works! No special handling needed.

For "has previously approved the demo client": do the full flow twice — first login (account created, no consent), second login (consent shown, click Approve), then reset context for the third login (consent skipped).

## Dont
- Do NOT check for literal "access_denied" in the URL — the demo converts it to "auth_failed"
- Do NOT use the literal Gherkin email strings — always use this.testEmail
- Do NOT use arrow functions for step definitions
- Do NOT modify the feature file (except adding @pending tag to scenario 5)
close_reason: 91560a2 Add consent-screen step definitions to auth.steps.ts

title: Extend auth.steps.ts — login-hint-resolution.feature step definitions
description:
## Files
- e2e/step-definitions/auth.steps.ts (modify)

## What to do
Add step definitions for login-hint-resolution.feature scenarios 1-3 and 5. Scenario 4 (PAR body hint) is tagged @pending.

### Key context
- The demo app accepts `?email=<value>` on its login API route: `GET /api/oauth/login?email=<value>`
- This causes the demo to send `login_hint=<email>` in the redirect to the auth service
- For handle hints, the demo accepts `?handle=<value>`: `GET /api/oauth/login?handle=<value>`
- The demo does NOT accept `?login_hint=` directly
- When login_hint is present, the auth service renders #step-otp.active immediately (skipping the email form)
- When login_hint is unknown/unresolvable, the auth service shows the email form (#step-email visible, #step-otp hidden)
- The Background step `{string} has a PDS account with handle {string}` (from task .1) creates an account and captures the real handle and DID on world

### Steps to implement

1. `When the demo client initiates OAuth with login_hint={string}`
   - Parse the hint value from the Gherkin string (e.g., "alice@example.com", "alice.pds.test", "did:plc:alice123")
   - Determine the hint type:
     - If it looks like an email (contains @): navigate to `demoUrl + "/api/oauth/login?email=" + encodeURIComponent(this.testEmail)` — use the REAL test email from world, not the literal Gherkin email
     - If it looks like a handle (contains . but no @): navigate to `demoUrl + "/api/oauth/login?handle=" + encodeURIComponent(this.userHandle!)` — use the REAL handle from world
     - If it starts with "did:": navigate to `demoUrl + "/api/oauth/login?handle=" + encodeURIComponent(this.userDid!)` — the demo resolves DIDs via the handle path. Actually, check if the demo supports DID login_hint... The demo login route only accepts ?email= or ?handle=. A DID would go through the handle path. But the demo resolves handles via PLC directory, not DIDs directly. For DID hints, we may need to go directly to the auth service URL.
     - RESOLUTION for DID: The demo does not support DID login hints. For the DID scenario, return "pending" — it would require constructing a PAR request manually and navigating to the auth service directly with login_hint=did:xxx. Mark this scenario @pending.
   - Wait for the auth service page to load

2. `Then the login page renders directly at the OTP verification step`
   - Assert `#step-otp.active` is visible (timeout 30s)
   - Assert `#step-email` is NOT visible (or has class hidden)

3. `And an OTP email is auto-sent to {string}`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Poll Mailpit for email to this.testEmail (the real email, not the literal Gherkin string)
   - Store OTP code on world

4. `Given alice's DID is {string}`
   - No-op. The real DID is already captured on this.userDid from the Background step. The literal Gherkin DID is ignored.

5. `When the demo client submits login_hint in the PAR request body (not the redirect URL)`
   - Return "pending" — this requires constructing a custom PAR request with DPoP

6. `Then the auth service retrieves the hint from the stored PAR request`
   - Return "pending"

7. `And the login page renders at the OTP step with the hint resolved`
   - Return "pending"

8. `Then the login page shows the email input form (hint could not be resolved)`
   - Assert `#email` is visible (the email input form is shown)
   - Assert `#step-otp` is NOT visible or does not have class active

### Scenario breakdown
- Scenario 1 (email hint): Navigate via `?email=<testEmail>` → assert OTP step visible. Works.
- Scenario 2 (handle hint): Navigate via `?handle=<testHandle>` → assert OTP step visible. Works.
- Scenario 3 (DID hint): Tag @pending — demo does not support DID login hints directly.
- Scenario 4 (PAR body hint): Tag @pending — needs custom PAR client.
- Scenario 5 (unknown hint): Navigate via `?handle=unknown.pds.test` → assert email form visible. Works (no account creation needed).

## Dont
- Do NOT use the literal Gherkin email/handle/DID strings — use this.testEmail, this.userHandle, this.userDid
- Do NOT navigate to the demo homepage for login_hint tests — use the /api/oauth/login route directly
- Do NOT try to construct PAR requests manually
- Do NOT use arrow functions for step definitions
close_reason: de8db47 Add login-hint-resolution step definitions to auth.steps.ts

title: Create account.steps.ts — account-settings.feature step definitions
description:
## Files
- e2e/step-definitions/account.steps.ts (create)

## What to do
Create step definitions for account-settings.feature. This feature uses a SEPARATE login flow from OAuth — the account settings login at /account/login uses server-rendered forms (POST /account/send-otp → POST /account/verify-otp), not the SPA-style OAuth flow.

### Key context — account settings login flow
1. Navigate to `authUrl + "/account/login"`
2. Page shows email form with `#email` input, submit button "Continue with email"
3. Fill email, submit → POST /account/send-otp → renders OTP form
4. OTP form has `#otp` input (NOT #code like the OAuth flow!), submit button "Verify"
5. Fill OTP, submit → POST /account/verify-otp → redirects to /account
6. /account page shows: DID in `<code>` element, email, handle input `[name=handle]`, backup email section, sessions section, danger zone with delete

### Key context — account settings page HTML
- DID displayed in `<code>` element containing "did:"
- Primary email displayed after "Primary Email:" label
- Handle update form: `<input name="handle">` + `.handle-suffix` showing ".{domain}" + submit button
- Backup email section: `<input name="email" type="email">` + "Add backup email" button
- Sessions section: session rows with revoke buttons
- Danger zone: `<details>` with `<summary>` "Delete account...", confirm input `[name=confirm]` with pattern="DELETE", delete button

### Helper function
Create and export a reusable helper:
```ts
export async function loginToAccountSettings(
  world: EpdsWorld,
  email: string,
): Promise<void> {
  // 1. Navigate to authUrl + "/account/login"
  // 2. Fill #email with email, submit form
  // 3. Wait for OTP form (#otp visible)
  // 4. Fetch OTP from Mailpit
  // 5. Fill #otp (NOT #code), submit form
  // 6. Wait for redirect to /account
  // 7. Assert h1 contains "Account Settings"
}
```

### Steps to implement

**Authentication:**
1. `When a user navigates to https://auth.pds.test/account without a session`
   - Navigate to `testEnv.authUrl + "/account"` (ignore the literal Gherkin URL)
   
2. `Then the browser is redirected to /account/login`
   - Assert URL ends with `/account/login`

3. `When the user navigates to /account/login`
   - Navigate to `testEnv.authUrl + "/account/login"`

4. `Then a login form is displayed (separate from the OAuth flow)`
   - Assert `#email` is visible
   - Assert page title or h1 contains "Account Settings"

5. `When the user enters {string} and verifies the OTP`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Call loginToAccountSettings helper

6. `Then the browser is redirected to /account`
   - Assert URL ends with `/account`

7. `And the account settings dashboard is displayed`
   - Assert h1 contains "Account Settings"

**Account information:**
8. `Given {string} is logged into account settings`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Create account via createAccountViaOAuth (from task .1) if not already created
   - Call loginToAccountSettings with this.testEmail

9. `When they view the /account page`
   - No-op (already on /account from the login step)

10. `Then the page displays their DID`
    - Assert `code` element contains "did:"

11. `And the page displays their primary email (masked)`
    - Assert page body contains masked email pattern (e.g., "t***@example.com" or similar)

12. `And the page displays their current handle`
    - Assert page contains a handle-like string (alphanumeric + ".") or the handle input is visible

**Handle management:**
13. `And their current handle is a random subdomain like {string}`
    - No-op (handle was auto-generated, we just note this)

14. `When the user submits a new handle {string}`
    - Fill `[name=handle]` with a unique handle (e.g., "e2e-" + Date.now()), NOT the literal Gherkin string
    - Submit the handle form
    - Wait for page reload/response

15. `Then the handle is updated to {string}`
    - Assert page contains the new handle text or success indication

16. `And the settings page reflects the new handle`
    - Assert the handle section shows the updated handle

17. `And https://{handle}/.well-known/atproto-did returns alice's DID`
    - Return "pending" — requires handle subdomain DNS resolution on Railway

**Session management:**
18. `And has at least one other active session`
    - No-op (the OAuth login from createAccountViaOAuth created one session, the account settings login created another)

19. `When the user views the sessions section`
    - Assert sessions section heading "Active Sessions" is visible

20. `When the user revokes another session`
    - Find a session row that is NOT the current session, click its revoke button

21. `Then active sessions are listed`
    - Assert at least one session row is visible

22. `Then that session is no longer listed`
    - Assert the revoked session row is gone (count decreased by 1)

**Account deletion:**
23. `When the user initiates account deletion and confirms`
    - Click the "Delete account..." summary to open the details
    - Fill `[name=confirm]` with "DELETE"
    - Click the delete button

24. `Then the browser is redirected away from /account (signed out)`
    - Assert URL does NOT contain "/account" (or contains "/account/login")

25. `And the PDS account for {string} no longer exists`
    - Return "pending" — would need internal API access or a login attempt that fails

26. `And com.atproto.server.getSession fails for alice's DID`
    - Return "pending" — needs bearer token

## Dont
- Do NOT use #code for the OTP input — account settings uses #otp
- Do NOT use the OAuth login flow for account settings — use /account/login
- Do NOT use literal Gherkin URLs (https://auth.pds.test) — use testEnv.authUrl
- Do NOT use literal Gherkin handles — generate unique handles
- Do NOT use arrow functions for step definitions
close_reason: d403274 feat: create account.steps.ts with account-settings step definitions

title: Extend email.steps.ts — email-delivery.feature step definitions
description:
## Files
- e2e/step-definitions/email.steps.ts (modify)

## What to do
Add step definitions for email-delivery.feature (3 scenarios). Most of the infrastructure already exists in email.steps.ts — this task extends it with new steps.

### Key context
- Existing email.steps.ts already has: `an OTP email arrives in the mail trap for {string}`, `an OTP email arrives in the mail trap`, subject assertion steps
- mailpit.ts already has: waitForEmail, extractOtp, clearMailpit
- Task .1 adds getMessageBody to mailpit.ts and lastEmailBody to EpdsWorld
- The email-delivery.feature Background has `a mail trap is capturing outbound emails` — this needs a new step

### Steps to implement

1. `Given a mail trap is capturing outbound emails`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit configured (this.skipIfNoMailpit())
   - This is a Background step that gates all scenarios in email-delivery.feature

2. `Then an email arrives in the mail trap addressed to {string}`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Poll Mailpit for email to the given address (use this.testEmail if the Gherkin email is a placeholder like "alice@example.com")
   - Store message on world (lastEmailSubject, lastEmailBody via getMessageBody)
   - Store otpCode via extractOtp

3. `And the email subject contains {string}`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Assert this.lastEmailSubject contains the expected string (case-insensitive)
   - NOTE: This step text does NOT have the "(new user)" or "(returning user)" suffix — it is a plain subject check. The existing steps have escaped parenthetical suffixes. This is a NEW step pattern.

4. `And the email body contains a numeric OTP code`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Assert this.lastEmailBody matches a numeric OTP pattern (e.g., /\d{4,12}/)

5. `Given {string} has an existing PDS account`
   - Already defined in task .1 (common.steps.ts)

6. `Then a verification email arrives in the mail trap for {string}`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Poll Mailpit for email to the given address
   - Store message on world

7. `And the email contains a verification link`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Assert this.lastEmailBody contains a URL with "/account/backup-email/verify"

8. `Given {string} is logged into account settings`
   - Already defined in task .5 (account.steps.ts)

9. `When the user adds {string} as a backup email`
   - Fill the backup email input `[name=email]` in the backup email section with the given email
   - Submit the backup email form (click "Add backup email" button)
   - Wait for page response

### Scenario 3 dependency
Scenario 3 (backup email verification link) requires being logged into account settings. This depends on task .5 (account.steps.ts) providing the `{string} is logged into account settings` step. If task .5 is not complete, this scenario will have undefined steps.

## Dont
- Do NOT duplicate existing step definitions — reuse the ones already in email.steps.ts
- Do NOT use arrow functions
- Do NOT hardcode Mailpit URLs — use the helpers from mailpit.ts

title: Create pds.steps.ts — automatic-account-creation + pds-behavior-at-risk browser/token scenarios
description:
## Files
- e2e/step-definitions/pds.steps.ts (create)

## What to do
Create step definitions for automatic-account-creation.feature and the browser/token-dependent scenarios from pds-behavior-at-risk.feature. Token-dependent scenarios return "pending".

### Key context
- The demo welcome page shows DID and handle (no access token in DOM)
- DID is rendered in a div with monospace font, text like "did:plc:abc123"
- Handle is rendered as "@<handle>" in a div
- No bearer token is accessible from the browser — Playwright network interception is deferred
- The Background step `a user account has been auto-created via the ePDS OAuth flow` (from task .1) creates an account and captures DID + handle on world

### Steps for automatic-account-creation.feature

1. `Given no PDS account exists for {string}`
   - Already defined in task .1 (common.steps.ts) — stores unique email on this.testEmail

2. `When {string} authenticates via the demo client`
   - Return pending if no Mailpit
   - Navigate to demoUrl, fill #email with this.testEmail, submit
   - Wait for #step-otp.active
   - Fetch OTP from Mailpit, fill #code, click verify
   - Wait for **/welcome (30s)
   - Capture DID and handle from welcome page, store on world

3. `Then the demo client receives a valid OAuth access token`
   - Assert welcome page body contains "did:" (proves token exchange succeeded)

4. `And the access token can be used to call com.atproto.server.getSession`
   - Return "pending" — needs bearer token

5. `And the response contains a DID and a handle matching *.pds.test`
   - Return "pending" — needs bearer token for getSession call
   - (Alternative: assert this.userDid starts with "did:" and this.userHandle contains "." — but this is from the welcome page, not getSession)

6. `Given {string} just had an account auto-created`
   - No-op if this.userDid is set (account was just created in previous scenario)
   - Otherwise return pending

7. `When the account handle is inspected via com.atproto.server.getSession`
   - Return "pending" — needs bearer token

8. `Then the handle is a short alphanumeric subdomain of the PDS domain`
   - If this.userHandle is set (from welcome page): assert it matches pattern /^[a-z0-9]+\.[a-z0-9.-]+$/i
   - Otherwise return "pending"

9. `And the handle subdomain resolves via HTTPS (TLS certificate provisioned)`
   - Return "pending" — needs DNS resolution for handle subdomain on Railway

10. `Given {string} has an auto-created PDS account`
    - Same as "has an existing PDS account" from task .1

11. `When the user creates a record via com.atproto.repo.createRecord`
    - Return "pending" — needs bearer token

12. `Then the record is created successfully`
    - Return "pending"

13. `And it can be retrieved via com.atproto.repo.getRecord`
    - Return "pending"

14. `When someone attempts com.atproto.server.createSession with any password`
    - Already defined in api.steps.ts (task .2) as the createSession step

15. `Then authentication fails`
    - Already defined in api.steps.ts (task .2) as "the request fails with an authentication error"

16. `And the only way to authenticate is through the ePDS OAuth flow`
    - No-op assertion (this is a documentation statement, not testable)

### Steps for pds-behavior-at-risk.feature (browser/token-dependent)

17. `When a user authenticates through the ePDS OAuth flow`
    - Return pending if no Mailpit
    - Same as step 2 (full login flow)

18. `And the demo client exchanges the authorization code for tokens`
    - No-op (the demo app does this automatically during the OAuth callback)

19. `Then the access token can be used to call authenticated XRPC endpoints`
    - Assert welcome page body contains "did:" (proves the token worked)

20. `When an OAuth client sends a PAR request to POST /oauth/par`
    - Return "pending" — needs DPoP key generation

21. `Then a valid request_uri is returned`
    - Return "pending"

22. `And the request_uri can be used in the authorization flow`
    - Return "pending"

23. `Given the user has a valid access token`
    - Return "pending" — no token available

24-29. All repo CRUD steps (createRecord, getRecord, deleteRecord)
    - Return "pending" — need bearer token

30. `Given the user's account was auto-created with a PLC DID`
    - Assert this.userDid starts with "did:plc:" (if set), otherwise pending

31. `When the DID document is resolved`
    - Return "pending" — needs PLC directory fetch

32-33. DID document assertions
    - Return "pending"

34. `Given the user has handle {string}`
    - Store this.userHandle (already set from account creation)

35. `When a client fetches https://{handle}/.well-known/atproto-did`
    - Return "pending" — needs handle subdomain DNS

36. `Then the correct DID is returned`
    - Return "pending"

37. `Given the user has created some records`
    - Return "pending" — needs bearer token

38. `When a client calls com.atproto.sync.getRepo for the user's DID`
    - Return "pending"

39. `Then the repo CAR file is returned successfully`
    - Return "pending"

## Dont
- Do NOT try to extract bearer tokens from Playwright network responses
- Do NOT import @atproto/api or any AT Protocol client library
- Do NOT use arrow functions for step definitions
- Do NOT duplicate steps already defined in api.steps.ts or common.steps.ts
close_reason: 6699ae1 Create pds.steps.ts for automatic-account-creation + pds-behavior-at-risk

title: Update cucumber.mjs paths to include all 7 new feature files and tag @pending scenarios
description:
## Files
- e2e/cucumber.mjs (modify)
- features/consent-screen.feature (modify — add @pending tag)
- features/login-hint-resolution.feature (modify — add @pending tag)
- features/oauth-metadata-override.feature (modify — add @pending tag)

## What to do

### 1. Update cucumber.mjs paths
Change the paths array from:
```js
paths: ["features/passwordless-authentication.feature"],
```
To:
```js
paths: [
  "features/passwordless-authentication.feature",

  "features/consent-screen.feature",

  "features/automatic-account-creation.feature",
  "features/pds-behavior-at-risk.feature",
],
```

### 2. Update tags to exclude @pending
Change:
```js
tags: "not @manual",
```
To:
```js
tags: "not @manual and not @pending",
```

### 3. Tag specific scenarios as @pending in feature files

**consent-screen.feature** — add `@pending` before Scenario 5:
```gherkin
  @pending
  Scenario: Consent page shows client branding for trusted clients
```
Reason: CSS injection feature branch not merged.

**login-hint-resolution.feature** — add `@pending` before Scenarios 3 and 4:
```gherkin
  @pending
  Scenario: DID login hint is resolved and skips the email form
```
Reason: Demo app does not support DID login hints.

```gherkin
  @pending
  Scenario: Login hint from PAR body is used when not on query string
```
Reason: Needs custom PAR client with DPoP.

**oauth-metadata-override.feature** — add `@pending` before Scenario 3:
```gherkin

  Scenario: OAuth clients discover and use the auth service automatically
```
Reason: Needs custom PAR client to initiate the full discovery flow.

### 4. Do NOT tag scenarios in these files
- automatic-account-creation.feature — token-dependent steps return "pending" at runtime (strict: false handles this)
- pds-behavior-at-risk.feature — same, runtime pending
- account-settings.feature — same, runtime pending
- email-delivery.feature — Mailpit guard handles this at runtime

The distinction: use @pending tag when the ENTIRE scenario cannot run (missing infrastructure). Use runtime "pending" return when individual steps within an otherwise-runnable scenario need deferral.

## Dont
- Do NOT add feature files that have no step definitions (security, tls, client-branding, social-login, account-recovery, epds-callback, internal-api)
- Do NOT change strict: false — it is needed for runtime pending steps
- Do NOT modify any step definition files in this task
- Do NOT remove the @risk-of-disruption tags from pds-behavior-at-risk.feature or oauth-metadata-override.feature

title: Fix: unused 'session' variable in POST /account/handle and POST /account/delete (from ePDS-01p.3)
description: Review of ePDS-01p.3 found: The refactor moved DID lookup into the requireBetterAuth middleware (injected via res.locals.did), but the POST /account/handle (line 289) and POST /account/delete (line 349) handlers in account-settings.ts still declare 'const session = res.locals.betterAuthSession' and never use it. This causes two @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars lint errors that fail 'pnpm lint'. Evidence: running 'pnpm lint' reports: account-settings.ts:289:13 error 'session' is assigned a value but never used; account-settings.ts:349:13 error 'session' is assigned a value but never used. Fix: remove the unused 'const session = ...' lines from both handlers (the DID is now available via res.locals.did, and the email is not needed in those handlers).

title: Replace /_magic/check-email with protected /_internal/account-by-email
description:
## Problem

The `/_magic/check-email` endpoint on pds-core returns `{ exists: true/false, did: "..." }` for any email with no authentication. It is an email enumeration oracle. It also reads from the `account_email` mirror table in `magic-pds.sqlite`, which we are eliminating as part of the broader migration to use `account.sqlite` as the single source of truth.

**Identified in the [magic-pds security review](https://gist.github.com/s-adamantine/c88df4fc940259cfe903e9de474f07fc#critical-vulnerabilities) as MED-4.**

See also: `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 0.4

## Solution

Replace `/_magic/check-email` with `/_internal/account-by-email`, which:
- Requires `x-internal-secret` header matching `MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET` env var
- Queries `pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail()` directly — the upstream PDS already has this as a public method on AccountManager (in `node_modules/@atproto/pds/src/account-manager/account-manager.ts`). It queries `account.sqlite` directly — no mirror table needed.
- Returns `{ did: string } | { did: null }`

## Implementation

1. In `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts`, add the new endpoint:

```typescript
app.get("/_internal/account-by-email", async (req, res) => {
  if (req.headers["x-internal-secret"] !== process.env.MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET) {
    return res.status(401).json({ error: "Unauthorized" });
  }
  const email = req.query.email as string;
  if (!email) return res.status(400).json({ error: "Missing email" });
  const account = await pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail(email);
  res.json({ did: account?.did ?? null });
});
```

2. Update auth-service to call `/_internal/account-by-email` with `x-internal-secret` header instead of calling `/_magic/check-email`.

3. Delete `/_magic/check-email` from pds-core.

4. Add `MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET` to `.env.example`.

## Files to modify

- `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts` — add new endpoint, delete old one
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/` (any file calling check-email) — update to new endpoint
- `.env.example` — add MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `/_internal/account-by-email` requires x-internal-secret header
- [ ] Unauthenticated calls return 401
- [ ] Valid calls return `{ did: "..." }` or `{ did: null }`
- [ ] `/_magic/check-email` is deleted
- [ ] Auth-service uses the new endpoint successfully
close_reason: Added /_internal/account-by-email endpoint to pds-core requiring x-internal-secret header, querying pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail() directly. Deleted /_magic/check-email. Updated auth-service account-login.ts to call the new protected endpoint. Added MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET to .env.example.

title: Stop assigning random passwords to new accounts
description:
## Problem

Accounts are created with a random 64-byte hex password (`crypto.randomBytes(64).toString('hex')`) in two places:
- `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts:130`
- `packages/auth-service/src/lib/auto-provision.ts:20`

This password is never shown to users but is a valid credential for `com.atproto.server.createSession`. If it were ever leaked (logs, memory dump, DB access), it bypasses the entire auth service.

**Identified in the [magic-pds security review](https://gist.github.com/s-adamantine/c88df4fc940259cfe903e9de474f07fc#critical-vulnerabilities) as MED-3.**

See also: `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 0.3

## Solution

Pass `password: undefined` when creating accounts. The PDS `accountManager.createAccount()` already handles this — it sets `passwordScrypt` to `undefined`, meaning:
- `createSession` rejects login (no hash to compare against)
- Password reset has nothing to reset
- Users who later want password-based login can use the stock PDS `requestPasswordReset` → `resetPassword` flow to set one — this works even when no password was previously set

## Implementation

1. In `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts:130`, change `password: randomBytes(64).toString('hex')` to `password: undefined`
2. In `packages/auth-service/src/lib/auto-provision.ts:20`, same change
3. Verify the PDS handles passwordless accounts correctly — test that `createSession` returns a proper error (not a crash) when there is no password hash

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] New accounts are created with no password
- [ ] `createSession` returns a clear error for passwordless accounts (not a 500)
- [ ] `requestPasswordReset` + `resetPassword` can set a password on a previously passwordless account
- [ ] Existing login flow via OTP is unaffected
close_reason: Removed random password generation from both auto-provision.ts (removed password field from XRPC call entirely since lexicon accepts optional) and pds-core/src/index.ts (used undefined as unknown as string to bypass SignUpInput schema type since no runtime schema validation at direct call site). Also removed unused crypto import from both files.

title: Security: no network isolation — /_internal endpoints exposed on same port as public PDS traffic
description: Review of ePDS-01p found: the /_internal/* endpoints (account-by-email, account-by-handle, check-handle, ping-request, par-login-hint) are registered on the same Express app and port (3000) as all public PDS traffic. docker-compose.yml exposes port 3000 publicly: 'ports: - ${PDS_PORT:-3000}:3000'. The only protection is the x-internal-secret header check. This means: (1) Any internet host that can reach port 3000 can attempt to call /_internal/account-by-email. The secret check prevents success, but the endpoint is reachable and the 401 response confirms the endpoint exists. (2) If EPDS_INTERNAL_SECRET is ever leaked (logs, env dump, compromised CI), an attacker can enumerate all registered emails by calling /_internal/account-by-email directly from the internet. (3) DNS rebinding: a browser on the same network as the PDS could be tricked into making credentialed requests to /_internal/* — though the x-internal-secret header is not a cookie so this is lower risk. Recommended fix: bind /_internal/* routes to a separate internal-only port (e.g. 3009) that is NOT exposed in docker-compose ports, only reachable via Docker internal network. Alternatively, add IP allowlist middleware that rejects requests to /_internal/* from non-RFC1918 source IPs. Evidence: packages/pds-core/src/index.ts:445-573, docker-compose.yml:7.

title: CSP middleware DB lookup fires on every request including static assets and /api/auth/*
description:
The proposed fix for the CSP img-src bug (adding cookie→DB fallback in index.ts middleware) causes ctx.db.getAuthFlow() to be called on every single request to the auth service — including /static/* asset requests, /health, /metrics, and all /api/auth/* better-auth endpoints.

While better-sqlite3 is synchronous and fast (sub-millisecond), this is architecturally incorrect: the CSP middleware should not perform DB lookups for requests that will never render a logo image. The /api/auth/* routes in particular are mounted BEFORE the middleware (line 31 vs line 43 in index.ts), so the middleware does not even run for those — but /static/*, /health, and /metrics do pass through it.

Fix: Scope the DB lookup to only the routes that render HTML pages with logos. Options:
  a) Move the CSP middleware to only apply to specific route prefixes (/oauth/*, /auth/*) using app.use('/oauth', ...) and app.use('/auth', ...).
  b) Skip the DB lookup when req.path starts with /static or /health or /metrics.
  c) Extract a helper function buildImgSrc(req, db) and call it only from the HTML-rendering route handlers, not from global middleware.

Evidence: index.ts line 43 — middleware is app.use() with no path filter; line 31 — /api/auth/* is mounted before middleware so it bypasses it; line 38 — /static is mounted before middleware so it also bypasses it. Actually re-checking: app.use('/static', ...) at line 38 is a static file handler — Express will short-circuit and not call subsequent middleware for matched static files. But /health and /metrics at lines 82-106 DO pass through the CSP middleware, and any unmatched routes also pass through it.

title: Fix: testing-gaps.md not updated after ePDS-01p changes (from ePDS-01p)
description:
Review of ePDS-01p found: AGENTS.md requires keeping testing-gaps.md up to date when tests are added or new gaps are discovered. The ePDS-01p epic added new test cases to get-did-by-email.test.ts and better-auth-otp.test.ts, and introduced a new untested area (requireBetterAuth middleware). The testing-gaps.md coverage summary table was not updated to reflect:

1. auth-service/lib/ coverage improvement (get-did-by-email.ts now has full coverage)
2. New gap: requireBetterAuth middleware in account-settings.ts (0% coverage, unit-testable with mocks)

Fix: update docs/design/testing-gaps.md to reflect the current state after ePDS-01p.

title: Create SKILL.md for developing frontend login/signup flows against ePDS
description:
Create a Claude Code skill (SKILL.md) that helps AI agents implement AT Protocol OAuth login and signup flows against an ePDS instance.

The skill should live at .claude/skills/epds-login/SKILL.md in this repo, making it installable via:

  npx skills add <owner>/ePDS@epds-login

It should be authored following the skill-authoring conventions in ~/.GIT/adamspiers.org/ai-config/.claude/skills/skill-authoring/SKILL.md (progressive disclosure, YAML frontmatter, concise, gerundial name, etc).

Scope of the skill:
- Flow 1 (app has email form, passes login_hint to PAR)
- Flow 2 (app has login button, auth server shows email form)
- PAR request structure (endpoint, params, login_hint, DPoP)
- Authorization redirect (including login_hint passthrough for Flow 1)
- Token exchange (code + DPoP)
- Client metadata format (client_id URL, required fields)
- Handle format (random, not email-derived)
- Common pitfalls (e.g. missing login_hint in redirect, DPoP nonce handling)

Reference material to draw from:
- docs/flows.md (login flow descriptions)
- README.md (PAR request, client metadata, token exchange sections)
- docs/architecture.md (system overview)
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts (auth server behaviour)
- maearth-demo app at /data/projects/maearth-demo (working reference implementation)

The skill should use progressive disclosure: core PAR/redirect/token flow in SKILL.md body, with detailed reference files (references/flows.md, references/client-metadata.md, etc.) for deeper content. Keep SKILL.md body under 500 lines.
close_reason: SKILL.md complete at .agents/skills/epds-login/SKILL.md with all required sections: two flows, PAR/redirect/token code, client metadata, handle format, pitfalls table, and 3 progressive-disclosure reference files. Added packages/demo as reference alongside maearth-demo.

title: CRITICAL: Sign magic callback with HMAC-SHA256
description:
## Problem

The `/oauth/magic-callback` endpoint on pds-core accepts security-critical authorization decisions as plain, unsigned query parameters:

```
GET /oauth/magic-callback?request_uri=urn:...&email=victim@example.com&approved=1&new_account=0
```

An attacker who initiates a legitimate OAuth flow (getting a valid `request_uri` via PAR) can directly call the magic-callback with the victim's email instead of completing OTP verification. If the PDS port is reachable (misconfigured firewall, Docker port mapping, open redirect), any account can be taken over without any authentication factor.

**This is the most severe finding from the [magic-pds security review](https://gist.github.com/s-adamantine/c88df4fc940259cfe903e9de474f07fc#critical-vulnerabilities).**

See also: `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 0.1

## Solution

Add a shared HMAC-SHA256 secret between auth-service and pds-core. The auth-service computes a signature over the callback parameters and a timestamp, appending `&ts=...&sig=...` to the redirect URL. pds-core verifies the signature before issuing an authorization code.

## Implementation

1. Add `MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET` env var (shared by both services). Generate with `openssl rand -hex 32`.

2. In `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts`, add `signCallback()`:
   - Input: `{ request_uri, email, approved, new_account }` + secret
   - Compute `ts = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000)`
   - Payload: `[request_uri, email, approved, new_account, ts].join("\n")`
   - Return HMAC-SHA256 hex digest + timestamp

3. In `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts`, add `verifyCallback()`:
   - Input: same params + ts + sig + secret
   - Reject if timestamp older than 5 minutes or in the future
   - Recompute HMAC and compare with `crypto.timingSafeEqual()`
   - Return boolean

4. In auth-service `routes/verify-code.ts` and `routes/consent.ts`: call `signCallback()` when building the redirect URL to `/oauth/magic-callback`. Append `&ts=...&sig=...`.

5. In pds-core `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts`, in the `/oauth/magic-callback` handler: call `verifyCallback()` before any account operations. Return 403 on failure, 400 on expiry.

6. Update `.env.example` with `MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET`.

## Files to modify

- `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts` — add signCallback/verifyCallback
- `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts` — add HMAC verification at top of magic-callback handler
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/verify-code.ts` — sign redirect URL
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/consent.ts` — sign redirect URL
- `.env.example` — add MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Unsigned requests to `/oauth/magic-callback` return 403
- [ ] Requests with expired timestamps (>5 min) return 400
- [ ] Requests with valid signatures proceed normally
- [ ] Comparison uses timingSafeEqual (no timing side-channel)
- [ ] Existing OTP login flow works end-to-end with HMAC in place
close_reason: Implemented HMAC-SHA256 signing for magic-callback: added signCallback/verifyCallback to shared/crypto.ts, updated verify-code.ts and consent.ts to sign redirects, added verification in pds-core magic-callback handler (returns 403/400 on invalid/expired sig), added MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET to .env.example, and added 9 new tests covering all verification paths.

title: Fix: POST choose-handle error re-renders lose the random button for picker-with-random mode (from ePDS-cg8)
description: Review of 4246a86 found: All four error re-render paths in the POST /auth/choose-handle handler (invalid format, PDS unavailable ×2, handle taken) call renderChooseHandlePage(handleDomain, error, csrfToken) without the showRandomButton argument. This means a user in picker-with-random mode who submits an invalid or taken handle gets a re-rendered page with no 'Generate random handle' button, even though flow.handleMode is available in the result object at that point. The fix is to compute showRandomButton = result.flow.handleMode === 'picker-with-random' at the top of the POST handler (same as the GET handler does on line 176) and pass it to all four renderChooseHandlePage calls. Evidence: choose-handle.ts lines 252-258, 282-289, 295-302, 308-315 — all omit the fourth argument.

title: Fix: csrfToken accepted in renderLoginPage opts but never used (from ePDS-aq2.1)
description:
Review of ePDS-aq2.1 found: The renderLoginPage function accepts `csrfToken: string` in its opts type (line 204) and the route handler passes `csrfToken: res.locals.csrfToken` (line 185), but the csrfToken is never used anywhere in the HTML template or JavaScript.

Evidence:
- Line 204: `csrfToken: string` in opts type
- Line 185: `csrfToken: res.locals.csrfToken` passed to renderLoginPage
- No occurrence of csrfToken in the template body (grep confirms 0 uses in HTML/JS)
- The better-auth fetch calls (/api/auth/email-otp/send-verification-otp, /api/auth/sign-in/email-otp) do not include any CSRF header

Note: This is not a security hole because better-auth routes are mounted before the CSRF middleware (index.ts line 31 vs line 39), so they bypass CSRF validation entirely. However, the dead parameter is misleading and should either be used (if CSRF protection is desired for better-auth routes) or removed from the opts type.

Fix: Remove `csrfToken` from the renderLoginPage opts type and the call site, since it is unused. If CSRF protection for better-auth endpoints is desired in the future, that should be a separate issue.

title: Epic: Make OTP code length configurable via OTP_LENGTH env var
description:
## Why
The OTP code length is hardcoded to 8 digits across multiple files (better-auth config, HTML templates, client-side JS, subtitle text). Operators should be able to configure this via a single OTP_LENGTH env var.

## Success
- Setting OTP_LENGTH=6 produces 6-digit codes, 6-char input fields, and '6-digit' subtitle text everywhere.
- Default remains 8 if OTP_LENGTH is unset.
- Startup fails fast with a clear error if OTP_LENGTH is outside 4-12 range or non-numeric.

## Scope
- packages/auth-service (config, better-auth, 3 route files, 1 test file, .env.example)
- packages/shared (deprecate legacy generateOtpCode)
- Does NOT include updating documentation files (docs/, README, AGENTS.md) — that is a separate follow-up.

title: Add otpLength to AuthServiceConfig and validate in main()
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/context.ts (modify)
- packages/auth-service/src/index.ts (modify)

## What to do

### context.ts
Add `otpLength: number` to the `AuthServiceConfig` interface (after `dbLocation: string` on line 23).

### index.ts — main() (lines 113-133)
Add to the config object:
```ts
otpLength: parseInt(process.env.OTP_LENGTH || '8', 10),
```

Add validation immediately after the config object is built (before `runBetterAuthMigrations` on line 135):
```ts
if (isNaN(config.otpLength) || config.otpLength < 4 || config.otpLength > 12) {
  throw new Error(`Invalid OTP_LENGTH: must be between 4 and 12, got "${process.env.OTP_LENGTH}"`)
}
```

### index.ts — createAuthService() (lines 74-77)
Change line 77 from:
```ts
app.use(createAccountLoginRouter(betterAuthInstance))
```
to:
```ts
app.use(createAccountLoginRouter(betterAuthInstance, ctx))
```

This passes `ctx` to `createAccountLoginRouter` so it can read `ctx.config.otpLength`. The other two routers (`createLoginPageRouter`, `createRecoveryRouter`) already receive `ctx`.

Update the import if needed — `createAccountLoginRouter` signature change happens in a dependent task.

## Don't
- Don't modify any router factory signatures in this task (that's done in dependent tasks)
- Don't read OTP_LENGTH anywhere except main()
- Don't add otpLength to AuthServiceContext class — it stays on the config interface only
close_reason: 2b4a880 Add otpLength to AuthServiceConfig and validate in main()

title: Update better-auth.ts to read OTP_LENGTH from process.env
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts (modify)

## What to do

Change both hardcoded `otpLength: 8` values to read from `process.env.OTP_LENGTH`.

### Line 84 (inside runBetterAuthMigrations — the migration-only temp instance)
Change:
```ts
otpLength: 8,
```
to:
```ts
otpLength: parseInt(process.env.OTP_LENGTH ?? '8', 10),
```

Note: This instance only exists to run DB schema migrations. The otpLength value doesn't affect the schema — we change it purely for consistency so grep finds zero hardcoded `otpLength: 8`.

### Line 149 (inside createBetterAuth — the live instance)
Same change:
```ts
otpLength: parseInt(process.env.OTP_LENGTH ?? '8', 10),
```

This is the instance that actually generates OTP codes at runtime. This is the change that matters.

### Why process.env directly?
This file already reads 6+ env vars directly (DB_LOCATION, AUTH_HOSTNAME, SMTP_FROM_NAME, PDS_HOSTNAME, SESSION_EXPIRES_IN, SESSION_UPDATE_AGE). Reading OTP_LENGTH the same way is consistent with the existing pattern. The router factories use ctx.config.otpLength instead (handled in other tasks).

## Don't
- Don't add validation here — validation happens in index.ts main() (task ePDS-ipm.1)
- Don't change the function signatures of runBetterAuthMigrations or createBetterAuth
- Don't touch any other config values
close_reason: 0bb73a4 Update better-auth.ts to read OTP_LENGTH from process.env

title: Update login-page.ts to use configurable otpLength from ctx.config
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts (modify)

## What to do

### 1. Read otpLength from ctx.config
The `createLoginPageRouter(ctx: AuthServiceContext)` function already receives `ctx`. Inside the route handler (around line 176), read:
```ts
const otpLength = ctx.config.otpLength
```

### 2. Add otpLength to renderLoginPage opts
Add `otpLength: number` to the opts type at line 195. Pass it at the call site (line 177):
```ts
otpLength: ctx.config.otpLength,
```

### 3. Update HTML template (around line 323)
Change:
```html
maxlength="8" pattern="[0-9]{8}"
```
to:
```html
maxlength="${opts.otpLength}" pattern="[0-9]{${opts.otpLength}}"
```

### 4. Update placeholder (same line area)
Change:
```html
placeholder="00000000"
```
to:
```html
placeholder="${'0'.repeat(opts.otpLength)}"
```

### 5. Update client-side JS subtitle text
The rendered HTML contains inline JavaScript. At lines 364 and 496, there are:
```js
otpSubtitle.textContent = 'We sent an 8-digit code to ' + masked;
```
These need the digit count injected. Inject otpLength as a JS variable in the rendered script:
```js
const otpLength = ${opts.otpLength};
```
Then use it:
```js
otpSubtitle.textContent = 'We sent a ' + otpLength + '-digit code to ' + masked;
```

## Don't
- Don't change the createLoginPageRouter function signature — it already receives ctx
- Don't read process.env.OTP_LENGTH directly — use ctx.config.otpLength
- Don't modify any other route files
close_reason: 9ff4dd5 Use configurable otpLength from ctx.config in login-page.ts

title: Update recovery.ts to use configurable otpLength from ctx.config
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/recovery.ts (modify)

## What to do

### 1. Read otpLength from ctx.config
The `createRecoveryRouter(ctx: AuthServiceContext, auth: any)` already receives `ctx`. Read `ctx.config.otpLength` inside the router factory and pass it to all `renderOtpForm` calls.

### 2. Add otpLength to renderOtpForm opts
The `renderOtpForm` function (line 228) has opts type:
```ts
{ email: string; csrfToken: string; requestUri: string; error?: string }
```
Add `otpLength: number` to this type.

### 3. Thread otpLength through all 4 call sites

title: Deprecate legacy generateOtpCode in shared/crypto.ts
description:
## Files
- packages/shared/src/crypto.ts (modify)

## What to do

The `generateOtpCode()` function (around lines 37-43) is a legacy function that predates the better-auth migration. It hardcodes 8-digit OTP generation:
```ts
export function generateOtpCode(): { code: string; codeHash: string } {
  const num = crypto.randomInt(0, 100_000_000)
  const code = num.toString().padStart(8, '0')
  const codeHash = hashToken(code)
  return { code, codeHash }
}
```

It is no longer called anywhere in the codebase — better-auth's emailOTP plugin handles OTP generation now.

Add a `@deprecated` JSDoc tag to the function:
```ts
/**
 * Generate an 8-digit OTP code. Returns the code and its SHA-256 hash.
 *
 * @deprecated This function is superseded by better-auth's emailOTP plugin
 * which handles OTP generation, hashing, and storage internally. The plugin
 * is configured in packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts. Do not use this
 * function for new code.
 */
```

Do NOT delete the function or change its behavior — it may be imported by external consumers.

Also check if it's re-exported from the package's index/barrel file. If so, leave the re-export in place.

## Don't
- Don't delete the function
- Don't change its behavior or signature
- Don't remove any re-exports
close_reason: 77819c2 Deprecate legacy generateOtpCode in shared/crypto.ts

title: Update recovery.test.ts to use dynamic OTP length instead of hardcoded 8
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/__tests__/recovery.test.ts (modify)

## What to do

There are two test blocks that hardcode OTP length to 8:

### Block 1 (lines 122-128)
```ts
describe('Recovery flow: OTP pattern (8 digits)', () => {
  it('OTP sent by better-auth is 8 digits (configured in emailOTP plugin)', () => {
    const OTP_LENGTH = 8
    const pattern = new RegExp(`^[0-9]{${OTP_LENGTH}}$`)
    // ...
  })
})
```

### Block 2 (lines 136-142)
```ts
it('OTP entry form uses maxlength=8 and pattern [0-9]{8}', () => {
  const maxlength = 8
  const pattern = '[0-9]{8}'
  expect(maxlength).toBe(8)
  expect(pattern).toContain('8')
})
```

### Changes needed

For both blocks, read the OTP length from `process.env.OTP_LENGTH` with default 8:
```ts
const OTP_LENGTH = parseInt(process.env.OTP_LENGTH ?? '8', 10)
```

Update the describe/it descriptions to not hardcode '8':
- `'Recovery flow: OTP pattern'` (drop '8 digits')
- `'OTP sent by better-auth matches configured length'`
- `'OTP entry form uses configured maxlength and pattern'`

Update Block 2 assertions to use the dynamic value:
```ts
const maxlength = OTP_LENGTH
const pattern = `[0-9]{${OTP_LENGTH}}`
expect(maxlength).toBe(OTP_LENGTH)
expect(pattern).toContain(String(OTP_LENGTH))
```

## Don't
- Don't change any other test files
- Don't import from better-auth internals — just read process.env
close_reason: 627028c Use dynamic OTP_LENGTH in recovery tests instead of hardcoded 8
