title: Fix: stale 'as any' cast on createAccountLoginRouter in index.ts (from ePDS-ipm)
description:
Review of ePDS-ipm found: The 'as any' cast on createAccountLoginRouter (index.ts line 78) was added in ePDS-ipm.1 as a temporary measure while the signature was updated. ePDS-ipm.5 updated the signature to accept (auth: any, ctx: AuthServiceContext): Router, making the cast unnecessary. The cast now silently suppresses TypeScript's ability to catch future signature mismatches at the call site.

Evidence: index.ts line 78:
  app.use((createAccountLoginRouter as any)(betterAuthInstance, ctx))

account-login.ts line 24-27:
  export function createAccountLoginRouter(
    auth: any,
    ctx: AuthServiceContext,
  ): Router

The function signature is now fully typed. The cast should be removed so TypeScript can verify the call site. The eslint-disable comment on line 77 should also be removed.

Fix: change line 78 to:
  app.use(createAccountLoginRouter(betterAuthInstance, ctx))
and remove the eslint-disable comment on line 77.
close_reason: 8c1e4da fix: remove stale 'as any' cast on createAccountLoginRouter

title: Fix: pds-behavior-at-risk Background creates account for every scenario including HTTP-only ones (from ePDS-geiz)
description:
Review of ePDS-geiz found: pds-behavior-at-risk.feature Background includes:
  And a user account has been auto-created via the ePDS OAuth flow

This step (common.steps.ts line 143) calls createAccountViaOAuth() which requires Mailpit and drives a full browser OAuth flow. It runs before EVERY scenario in the feature, including:
- 'describeServer returns valid PDS metadata' (pure HTTP, no account needed)
- 'Password-based createSession is not possible' (pure HTTP, no account needed)

When Mailpit is not configured, the step returns 'pending' and the entire scenario is skipped — including the two HTTP-only scenarios that should run without Mailpit.

This means the two most valuable regression tests (describeServer and createSession) are silently skipped in environments without Mailpit, even though they have no email dependency.

Fix options:
1. Move the account creation Background step to only the scenarios that need it (using Scenario-level Given steps instead of Background).
2. Split the feature into two: one for HTTP-only scenarios (no Background account creation) and one for token-dependent scenarios.
3. Make the Background step a no-op when Mailpit is absent (don't return pending — just skip the account creation and let HTTP-only scenarios proceed).
close_reason: 1d2276a fix: move account creation out of Background in pds-behavior-at-risk

title: Design gap: scope estimate of 80-100 lines is too low — plan omits test updates and error message copy
description:
## Finding

The plan estimates ~80-100 lines across 3 files. Based on reading the actual source, the realistic scope is larger:

### recovery.ts changes
- GET handler: replace 10-line query-param block with cookie lookup (~same lines, but adds error path for missing cookie)
- POST /auth/recover: replace req.body reads with cookie lookup + DB call; remove hidden field rendering from renderRecoveryForm and renderOtpForm; update 'Back to sign in' href to read from DB at render time
- POST /auth/recover/verify: replace req.body reads with cookie lookup; add error handling for missing cookie
- renderRecoveryForm: remove 2 hidden input lines, update href construction
- renderOtpForm: remove 4 hidden input lines (2 forms × 2 fields), update href construction

Estimated: ~40-50 lines changed/removed in recovery.ts

### login-page.ts changes
- Line 368: simplify recovery link href (1 line)
- renderLoginPage opts: remove requestUri param if no longer needed in template (affects function signature + call site)

Estimated: ~5-10 lines

### consent.ts changes
- GET handler: remove legacy branch (lines 70-100, ~30 lines)
- POST handler: remove legacy branch (lines 139-149, ~10 lines)  
- renderConsent: remove conditional hidden fields (lines 207-215, ~8 lines)
- Update JSDoc comment

Estimated: ~50 lines removed

### Test updates (not mentioned in plan)
- recovery.test.ts: fix clientId assertion (issue ePDS-e56)
- Add route-level tests for cookie-based lookup paths
- Add test for missing-cookie error path in recovery GET

Estimated: ~30-50 lines

### Total realistic estimate: 125-160 lines across 4 files (including tests)

The plan should be updated to reflect this scope so the implementer allocates sufficient time.

title: CRITICAL: Increase OTP to 8 digits and add per-email lockout
description:
## Problem

The current OTP system uses 6 digits (1,000,000 possibilities) with 5 attempts per token and 5 tokens per email per hour. This allows 25 guesses/hour. Over days or weeks this becomes non-trivial for a targeted attack against a specific account.

**Identified in the [magic-pds security review](https://gist.github.com/s-adamantine/c88df4fc940259cfe903e9de474f07fc#critical-vulnerabilities) as CRITICAL-2.**

See also: `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 0.2

## Solution

1. Increase OTP from 6 to 8 digits (100,000,000 possibilities — 100x harder brute-force)
2. Add a per-email lockout after N total failed attempts across all tokens (e.g., 15 total failures in 1 hour → lock the email for 1 hour)

## Implementation

### 8-digit OTP (current codebase)

In `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts`, change `generateOtpCode()` to generate 8-digit codes instead of 6-digit codes. This is a single-line change to the `randomInt()` range.

Update all email templates in `packages/auth-service/src/email/sender.ts` to accommodate 8-digit codes (the letter-spacing and font-size in the HTML templates may need slight adjustment).

### 8-digit OTP (after better-auth migration)

Set `otpLength: 8` in the `emailOTP` plugin configuration in `packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts`.

### Per-email lockout

Add a new column or table tracking total failed attempts per email across all OTP tokens within a time window. When 15 failures occur within 1 hour, reject new OTP verification attempts for that email for 1 hour. This is orthogonal to the existing per-token attempt limit (5 per token).

## Files to modify

- `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts` — change OTP digit count
- `packages/auth-service/src/email/sender.ts` — update email templates for 8-digit display
- `packages/auth-service/src/magic-link/rate-limit.ts` or `packages/shared/src/db.ts` — add per-email lockout logic

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] OTP codes are 8 digits
- [ ] Email templates display 8 digits correctly
- [ ] Per-email lockout activates after 15 total failed attempts in 1 hour
- [ ] Lockout prevents new OTP verification (not sending — user can still request codes)
- [ ] Existing login flow works with 8-digit codes
close_reason: Increased OTP from 6 to 8 digits (100M possibilities), added per-email lockout (15 failures/hour → 1-hour lock) via new otp_failed_attempts table in db.ts with migration v4, updated token.ts to check and record failures, updated email templates for 8-digit display, added lockout test.

title: Fix: no unit tests for requireBetterAuth drift detection middleware (from ePDS-01p.3)
description:
Review of ePDS-01p.3 found: the requireBetterAuth middleware is the core of the drift detection feature but has zero unit test coverage. The three critical paths are untested:

1. PDS returns null (error) → should return 503, not touch session
2. PDS returns { did: null } (drift confirmed) → should call deleteUser, call signOut, redirect to /account/login
3. PDS returns { did: string } (valid) → should set res.locals.did and call next()

Additionally, the error handling sub-paths are untested:
- deleteUser throws → should log error but still redirect (not 500)
- signOut throws → should be silently ignored, still redirect

Per AGENTS.md, route handlers should be covered via supertest integration tests. The middleware is pure enough (takes auth, pdsUrl, internalSecret) that it could be tested with a mock auth object and a mocked fetch.

Suggested test file: packages/auth-service/src/__tests__/require-better-auth.test.ts
Use vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch') for PDS responses and a mock auth object with getSession/signOut/ stubs.

title: Fix: dark mode panel background not applied when AUTH_PANEL_COLOR is set (from ePDS-wae.10)
description: When AUTH_PANEL_COLOR is set, the rootStyle injects --color-panel:${panelColor} into :root inline style. The .title-panel background is set to var(--color-panel) in the >=768px media query (line 415). However, the dark mode override at line 430-434 only adds a border-right — it does NOT override the background. In dark mode with a custom panel color, the panel will show the custom color (correct), but the dark mode :root overrides --color-contrast-25 to a dark value. Since --color-panel defaults to var(--color-contrast-25), without a custom panel color the dark mode panel is correctly dark. With a custom panel color it is correctly the custom color. This is actually fine. BUT: the light-mode rule at line 436-441 uses var(--color-panel, hsl(...)) with a fallback — this fallback is redundant since --color-panel is always defined in :root. Minor: remove the redundant fallback to avoid confusion.

title: Allow apps to optionally let users choose a handle at signup
description: Currently, new users get an auto-generated handle when signing up through a third-party app. Add support for apps to optionally present a handle-selection step during signup, so users can choose their own handle (e.g. alice.their-pds.example) instead of getting a random one. This should be opt-in per app — apps that don't want this step skip it and get the current auto-generated behavior.

title: Security: client metadata brand_color/background_color not validated — CSS injection via OAuth client (from pre-deploy review)
description:
## Problem

Pre-deployment review found that `brand_color` and `background_color` from OAuth client metadata (fetched from `client_id` URL) are injected into CSS without hex-color validation.

In `packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts`:
```ts
// line 219-220
const brandColor = b.brand_color || opts.defaultBrandColor || '#8338ec'
const bgColor = b.background_color || opts.defaultBgColor || null

// line 239-240 — injected into style attribute
rootStyleProps += `--color-primary:${escapeHtml(brandColor)};...`

// line 250-252 — injected into <style> block
const bgColorStyle = bgColor
  ? `<style>body { background: ${escapeHtml(bgColor)} !important; }</style>`
  : ''
```

`escapeHtml()` only escapes HTML entities (`&`, `<`, `>`, `"`). It does NOT prevent CSS injection via `;`, `}`, `url(`.

A malicious OAuth client at `https://evil.com/client-metadata.json` could set:
```json
{ "background_color": "red} body::after{content:url(https://evil.com/x" }
```

## Mitigations already in place
- CSP `img-src 'self' data:` blocks external image exfiltration
- CSS custom properties in style attributes cannot contain `url()` in most browsers
- The env var path (AUTH_BRAND_COLOR etc.) IS already validated by `isValidHexColor()` (fixed in ePDS-3fc)

## Severity
P2 — requires a malicious OAuth client registration. CSP mitigates the worst exfiltration vectors. Not a deployment blocker.

## Fix
Apply the same `isValidHexColor()` validation to `b.brand_color` and `b.background_color` before using them in CSS. Fall back to defaults if invalid.

title: Smoke test demo frontend
description:
Verify the demo package builds and the login flow works. See docs/design/demo-frontend.md for full spec.

Steps:
- Run pnpm install from root
- Run pnpm dev:demo and verify Next.js starts on port 3002 (or whichever port)
- Verify no TypeScript errors (pnpm --filter @certified-app/demo exec next lint or similar)
- Verify / renders the login page with ePDS Demo branding
- Verify /flow2 renders the button-only login page
- Verify /client-metadata.json returns correct OAuth metadata with client_name 'ePDS Demo'
- If a running ePDS instance is available, test the full OAuth flow: login -> callback -> /welcome showing handle + DID
- Document any setup steps needed in .env.example comments
close_reason: Dev server starts clean on port 3002, all pages return 200, client-metadata.json correct, no compilation errors, no Ma Earth branding leaks. Added .next/ to .gitignore.

title: Build unified login page with multiple auth methods
description:
## Context

Phase 2 of the better-auth migration plan. See `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 2.4.

## Problem

The current auth-service login flow is hardcoded for email OTP only (authorize.ts → send-code.ts → verify-code.ts). It needs to become a landing page offering multiple login methods.

## Solution

Replace the existing `GET /oauth/authorize` on the auth-service with a unified login page that:

1. Receives `?request_uri=...&client_id=...&prompt=...&login_hint=...` from the pds-core AS metadata redirect
2. Creates an `auth_flow` row (flow_id, request_uri, client_id)
3. Sets `magic_auth_flow` cookie (10 min, httpOnly)
4. Resolves client metadata for branding via existing `lib/client-metadata.ts` (logo, colors, name)
5. Renders a login page with:
   - Email OTP form (calls better-auth `/api/auth/*` endpoints on auth-service)
   - Social login buttons (only for providers enabled via env vars — check exported `socialProviders` object from `better-auth.ts`)
   - "Recover with backup email" link (to existing recovery flow)

### Social provider buttons

The login page template conditionally renders buttons based on `socialProviders`:
```typescript
if ("google" in socialProviders) { /* render Google button */ }
if ("github" in socialProviders) { /* render GitHub button */ }
```

Social login redirects to better-auth `/api/auth/sign-in/{provider}`, which handles the full OAuth exchange and redirects back to `/auth/complete` (the bridge route).

### Delete on completion

After this is working, delete the old files:
- `routes/authorize.ts`, `routes/send-code.ts`, `routes/verify-code.ts`
- `magic-link/token.ts`, `magic-link/rate-limit.ts`
- `middleware/rate-limit.ts`, `middleware/csrf.ts`
- Drop `magic_link_token` table from auth-service SQLite

Also remove `tokenService` and `rateLimiter` from `context.ts`.

## Files to create

- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts` — unified login page

## Files to modify

- `packages/auth-service/src/index.ts` — mount new /oauth/authorize, remove old routes
- `packages/auth-service/src/context.ts` — remove tokenService, rateLimiter

## Files to delete

- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/authorize.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/send-code.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/verify-code.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/magic-link/token.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/magic-link/rate-limit.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/middleware/rate-limit.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/middleware/csrf.ts`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Login page renders with email OTP form
- [ ] Social login buttons appear only for configured providers
- [ ] Client branding (logo, name, colors) displayed from OAuth metadata
- [ ] Email OTP flow works end-to-end through better-auth
- [ ] Social login (if configured) works end-to-end through better-auth → /auth/complete bridge
- [ ] Old OTP route files deleted
- [ ] Old middleware files deleted
- [ ] context.ts cleaned up
close_reason: Created routes/login-page.ts: unified GET /oauth/authorize that creates auth_flow row, sets magic_auth_flow cookie, renders login page with better-auth email OTP form (JavaScript fetch to /api/auth/* endpoints) and optional social provider buttons (Google/GitHub via env vars). Client branding applied. Deleted old authorize.ts, send-code.ts, verify-code.ts. Removed tokenService/rateLimiter from context.ts, deleted magic-link/ directory. 10 login-page tests added, 83 tests total pass.

title: random-mode path deletes auth_flow before redirect, leaving no retry path on pds-core failure
description: Review of 0962a39 found: In complete.ts step 5a (random mode), the auth_flow row is deleted and the cookie is cleared BEFORE the redirect to /oauth/epds-callback (lines 110-111, then redirect at line 128). The comment says 'there is no retry path' as justification. However, if the epds-callback redirect fails (network error, pds-core 500, expired request_uri), the user has no way to retry — the auth_flow is gone, the cookie is cleared, and the browser will land on an error page with no recovery path. By contrast, the existing-user path (step 5d) also deletes before redirect, but that path has no account-creation side effects. For the random-mode new-account path, a failure after deletion leaves the user in a broken state: no PDS account, no auth_flow, no cookie. The safer pattern is to delete auth_flow only after a successful response from pds-core (or accept that TTL cleanup will handle it). Evidence: packages/auth-service/src/routes/complete.ts lines 110-131.

title: Epic: Replace auth-service consent page with stock @atproto OAuth consent UI
description:
## Goal
Replace the handcrafted consent page in auth-service with the stock @atproto/oauth-provider React authorization UI. After OTP verification, auth-service redirects to a new /oauth/epds-setup endpoint on pds-core, which handles account setup and then renders the stock consent page with the user's account pre-selected.

## Why
The current consent page has critical gaps:
1. Hardcoded permissions — always shows 'Read and write posts / Access your profile / Manage your follows' regardless of actual requested scopes. A malicious client could request atproto:admin and the user would never know.
2. No security warnings for dangerous scopes.
3. No client branding (logo, ToS, privacy links).
4. No i18n (English-only vs 36 locales in stock page).
5. Missing security headers (CSP, fetch metadata validation, cache-control).
6. Two separate consent tracking systems (client_logins table vs stock authorizedClients) that can desync.

## What success looks like
- New users: OTP → stock consent page (real scopes, client info) → redirect
- Existing users, new client: OTP → stock consent page → redirect
- Existing users, returning client: OTP → stock consent page → redirect (spec-correct: prompt=consent forced for public clients)
- The stock React app handles code issuance, consent recording, CSRF, redirect construction — we don't implement any of that.

## Key constraints
- One internal import from @atproto/oauth-provider (sendAuthorizePageFactory) — pinned to ^0.15.9, must check on upgrades.
- provider.requestManager accessed via 'as any' cast (existing pattern).
- Returning users now always see consent (AT Protocol spec-correct behavior, UX improvement with real scopes).
- Keep /oauth/epds-callback temporarily until new route is confirmed working.

## Scope
4 files changed: pds-core/src/index.ts (add route), auth-service/src/routes/complete.ts (simplify), auth-service/src/routes/consent.ts (delete), auth-service/src/index.ts (remove consent router).

title: Add GET /oauth/epds-setup route to pds-core
description:
## Files
- packages/pds-core/src/index.ts (modify)

## What to do

Add a new `GET /oauth/epds-setup` route handler to pds-core. This route replaces the consent page flow — it receives an HMAC-signed redirect from auth-service after OTP verification, sets up the account/device, then renders the stock @atproto consent page.

### New import (add near top of file, after existing imports)

```ts
// Internal import — pinned to @atproto/oauth-provider@^0.15.x
// Must be verified on @atproto version bumps.
// eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/ban-ts-comment
// @ts-ignore — internal module, no type declarations exported
import { sendAuthorizePageFactory } from "@atproto/oauth-provider/dist/router/assets/send-authorization-page.js"
```

### Factory initialization (inside the `if (provider)` block, around line 48, before any route definitions)

```ts
const issuerUrl = new URL(pdsUrl)
const sendAuthorizePage = sendAuthorizePageFactory(provider.customization, {
  hsts: issuerUrl.protocol === "http:" ? false : undefined,
})
```

### Route handler: `pds.app.get("/oauth/epds-setup", async (req, res) => { ... })`

Place it AFTER the factory initialization, BEFORE the existing `/oauth/epds-callback` route.

The handler has two phases:

**Phase A — Account setup (reuse logic from existing epds-callback):**

1. Parse query params: `request_uri`, `email`, `ts`, `sig`. Note: NO `approved` param needed (it was only used by the old consent flow).
2. Validate required params: `request_uri`, `email` must be present. `ts` and `sig` must be present.
3. Verify HMAC signature using `verifyCallback()`. The `CallbackParams` object should use `approved: "1"` and `new_account: req.query.new_account as string` (auth-service still sends these for HMAC compatibility). Same error handling as existing callback (distinguish expired vs forged).
4. Check `provider` is available (same as existing callback).
5. Inside try/catch:
   a. `provider.deviceManager.load(req as unknown as http.IncomingMessage, res as unknown as http.ServerResponse)` → `{ deviceId, deviceMetadata }`
   b. `(provider.requestManager as any).get(requestUri, deviceId)` → `{ clientId, parameters }`
   c. `provider.clientManager.getClient(clientId)` → `client` (this is NEW vs old callback — needed because `sendAuthorizePage` requires the full Client object with `.metadata`, `.info.isTrusted`, `.info.isFirstParty`)
   d. Resolve or create account — EXACT same logic as existing epds-callback lines 134-183 (getAccountByEmail, createAccount with retry loop, generateRandomHandle, random password, invite code)
   e. `provider.accountManager.upsertDeviceAccount(deviceId, account.sub)` — link device to account

**Phase B — Render stock consent page:**

6. Call `provider.authorize()`:
   ```ts
   const query = { request_uri: requestUri, client_id: clientId }
   const result = await (provider as any).authorize(query, { deviceId, deviceMetadata })
   ```
   Note: cast provider to `any` because `authorize()` expects branded types for the query params.

7. Check if result is a redirect (auto-approve case):
   ```ts
   if ("redirect" in result) {
   ```
   If so, build the redirect URL from `result.parameters.redirect_uri` + `result.redirect.code` + state + iss. Use the same URL construction logic as existing epds-callback lines 212-241. Also record consent using the same logic as existing epds-callback lines 198-210 (checkConsentRequired + setAuthorizedClient). Then `res.redirect(303, url)`.

   This branch is unlikely to fire (prompt=consent is forced for public clients) but handle it for correctness.

8. Otherwise (authorize page result — the normal case):
   ```ts
   // Patch sessions to pre-select the authenticated user
   const patchedResult = {
     ...result,
     sessions: result.sessions.map((s: any) => ({
       ...s,
       selected: s.account.sub === did,
     })),
   }
   await sendAuthorizePage(
     req as unknown as http.IncomingMessage,
     res as unknown as http.ServerResponse,
     patchedResult,
   )
   ```

9. Error handling (catch block): Same pattern as existing epds-callback lines 244-275. Try to redirect error back to client redirect_uri. Fall back to 500 JSON.

### Important details
- The `did` variable from Phase A step (d) must be accessible in Phase B. Declare it with `let did: string | undefined` before the try block, same as existing callback.
- Add eslint-disable comments for `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any` on the `as any` casts, same pattern as existing code.
- Add a JSDoc comment block at the top of the route explaining the flow (same style as existing epds-callback comment block on lines 51-56).
- Set `res.setHeader("Cache-Control", "no-store")` before calling sendAuthorizePage (same as stock middleware does).

## Dont
- Do NOT delete the existing `/oauth/epds-callback` route. It stays for now.
- Do NOT import anything else from @atproto/oauth-provider internals besides `sendAuthorizePageFactory`.
- Do NOT modify the AS metadata override or any internal endpoints.
- Do NOT add new dependencies to package.json (oauth-provider is already a dependency).
- Do NOT try to update stored PAR parameters (login_hint injection). We patch `selected` on the result instead.
close_reason: 9b1ace2 Add GET /oauth/epds-setup route to pds-core

title: epds-setup: no test coverage for the new /oauth/epds-setup route
description: The new /oauth/epds-setup route in pds-core/src/index.ts has zero unit or integration tests. The consent.test.ts file was updated to reflect the new flow but only tests DB operations and HMAC signing — it does not test the route handler itself. Missing coverage includes: (1) HMAC signature validation (missing sig/ts → 403, expired → 400, invalid → 403), (2) missing required params → 400, (3) provider not configured → 500, (4) error redirect path (catch block redirects to client redirect_uri with server_error), (5) the auto-approve branch (result has 'redirect' key). The epds-callback route also has no route-level tests, but it predates this epic. At minimum, add unit tests for the validation/error paths using mock req/res objects (same pattern as login-page.test.ts).

title: stale epds-callback references in comments across auth-service and shared packages
description: Several comments still reference /oauth/epds-callback as the destination of the bridge flow, even though complete.ts now redirects to /oauth/epds-setup: (1) packages/auth-service/src/routes/recovery.ts line 12: 'better-auth session → /auth/complete → HMAC-signed epds-callback' (2) packages/auth-service/src/context.ts line 10: 'Shared HMAC-SHA256 secret for signing epds-callback redirect URLs' (3) packages/shared/src/crypto.ts lines 58 and 83: JSDoc for signCallback/verifyCallback still says 'epds-callback redirect' (4) packages/auth-service/src/__tests__/social-providers.test.ts lines 11 and 115: comments say 'epds-callback'. These are documentation-only issues with no runtime impact, but they will mislead future developers.

title: HMAC malleable: new_account from query string breaks HMAC integrity in /oauth/epds-setup
description: In complete.ts, new_account is hardcoded to '0' when signing the HMAC (line 93). In /oauth/epds-setup, the verifier reads new_account from the query string (req.query.new_account) and uses that value in the HMAC verification payload (line 107-113). This means an attacker who intercepts or crafts a URL can set new_account to any value and the signature will still verify — because the signer always uses '0' and the verifier uses whatever is in the URL. The HMAC payload is: request_uri + email + approved + new_account + ts. Since new_account is always '0' from the signer, the verifier should also hardcode '0' rather than reading it from the untrusted query string. As written, an attacker can change new_account=1 in the URL and the signature still verifies (because the verifier recomputes with the attacker-supplied value, which matches the signature computed with '0' only if the attacker also supplies '0' — wait, actually the attacker cannot forge a valid sig for a different new_account value without the secret). HOWEVER: the real risk is the inverse — the verifier trusts new_account from the URL as part of the verified payload, but the signer always sends '0'. If the verifier ever uses new_account for a security decision (e.g., skipping account creation checks), an attacker who replays a valid URL with new_account=1 would pass verification. Currently new_account is not used after verification in epds-setup, but the inconsistency is a latent vulnerability and violates the principle that all HMAC-covered fields should be read from the verified payload, not re-read from the untrusted input. Fix: hardcode new_account: '0' in the verifyCallback call in epds-setup, matching what complete.ts always signs.

title: CSRF flow broken: stock /~api/consent requires Referer: /oauth/authorize but epds-setup serves from /oauth/epds-setup
description: The stock @atproto /~api/consent endpoint (create-api-middleware.ts line 730-748) validates the Referer header and requires it to be from the same origin with pathname '/oauth/authorize'. It then extracts the request_uri from the Referer URL's search params to associate the consent POST with the pending authorization request. When sendAuthorizePage() is called from /oauth/epds-setup, the React app is served from that URL. When the user clicks Approve, the browser sends Referer: https://pds.example/oauth/epds-setup?... — NOT Referer: https://pds.example/oauth/authorize?.... The stock middleware will reject this with HTTP 400 'Invalid referrer'. This means the consent POST will always fail, breaking the entire new flow. The stock authorize page middleware (create-authorization-page-middleware.ts) serves the page from /oauth/authorize, which is why the Referer check works in the normal flow. Fix options: (a) Serve the page via a redirect to /oauth/authorize (preserving the request_uri in the URL so the Referer is correct), or (b) Override the /~api/consent endpoint to accept Referer from /oauth/epds-setup as well, or (c) Use a different approach to render the page that sets the correct URL context.

title: selected:true injection allows attacker to pre-select a different account on shared device
description: In /oauth/epds-setup Step 8, the code patches result.sessions to set selected:true on the session whose account.sub matches the HMAC-verified DID. However, if the device has multiple accounts (SSO scenario), all sessions are returned by provider.authorize() and the patch sets selected:true on the HMAC-verified account. The stock React consent UI uses the 'selected' flag to pre-select an account in the UI, but the user can still switch to a different account and approve as that account — the actual authorization is determined by the 'sub' field the React app sends to /~api/consent, not by the 'selected' flag. So the injection itself is not a security bypass. HOWEVER: there is a subtle risk. If an attacker can replay a valid HMAC-signed URL (within the 5-minute window) on a device where they have a session, they could trigger account creation for the victim email and link it to their device. The device cookie is loaded from the attacker's browser, so upsertDeviceAccount() links the victim's new account to the attacker's device. The attacker then sees the consent page with the victim's account pre-selected and their own account also listed. If they approve as themselves, no harm. But the victim's account is now linked to the attacker's device, enabling future SSO. Fix: after HMAC verification, verify that the email in the HMAC matches the currently authenticated session on the device (if any), or ensure upsertDeviceAccount is only called after the user explicitly consents via the stock UI.

title: Auto-approve redirect branch reads result.parameters but result shape is AuthorizationResultRedirect not AuthorizePage
description: In /oauth/epds-setup Step 7 (auto-approve redirect branch, lines 220-269), the code does: const redirectUri = result.parameters?.redirect_uri. Looking at the AuthorizationResultRedirect type (authorization-result-redirect.ts), the redirect result shape is { issuer, parameters, redirect: { code } }. So result.parameters IS present. However, the code then constructs the redirect URL manually, duplicating the logic from the existing epds-callback route. The problem is that the stock oauth-provider's authorize() method, when it returns a redirect result, has already computed the correct redirect URL internally via buildRedirectUrl(). The code bypasses this and re-implements URL construction, which could diverge from the stock implementation (e.g., missing response_mode=form_post handling, missing error_uri, missing custom parameters). Additionally, result.redirect.code is accessed but the AuthorizationResultRedirect type has redirect: { code: Code } where Code is a branded type — accessing it as a plain string may work at runtime but is fragile. The safer fix is to use the stock /oauth/authorize/redirect endpoint that the stock consent UI uses, rather than re-implementing redirect construction.

title: Account creation race: concurrent requests with same email create duplicate accounts
description: In /oauth/epds-setup Step 4, the account resolution is: (1) getAccountByEmail(email) — if null, (2) createAccount(). Between steps 1 and 2, a concurrent request for the same email (e.g., user double-clicking, or a replay within the 5-minute HMAC window) could also find no account and attempt createAccount(). The retry loop (3 attempts) only handles handle collisions, not email uniqueness violations. If two concurrent requests both pass getAccountByEmail returning null and both call createAccount with the same email, one will succeed and one will throw a unique constraint violation on the email column. The throwing request will propagate the error to the catch block and attempt to redirect to the client with server_error. The user sees an error page. The same race exists in the old epds-callback route, so this is not a regression, but it is worth noting. Fix: wrap the getAccountByEmail + createAccount sequence in a database transaction or use an email-keyed mutex/lock. Alternatively, catch the unique constraint error specifically and retry getAccountByEmail.

title: sendAuthorizePageFactory internal import: no version pin enforcement or breakage detection
description: The import 'import { sendAuthorizePageFactory } from @atproto/oauth-provider/dist/router/assets/send-authorization-page.js' is from an internal (non-exported) module path. The comment says 'pinned to @atproto/oauth-provider@^0.15.x, must be verified on @atproto version bumps' but there is no automated enforcement. If @atproto/pds is upgraded and pulls in a new @atproto/oauth-provider version that renames or removes this internal export, the build will fail at runtime (not at compile time, since the import is @ts-ignore'd). The function signature could also change silently. Risks: (1) The function is currently exported from the dist file and its signature matches what we call it with — verified against 0.15.10. (2) No CI check enforces the version constraint. (3) The @ts-ignore suppresses type errors that would otherwise catch signature changes. Mitigations to add: (a) Add a package.json overrides/resolutions entry to pin @atproto/oauth-provider to exactly 0.15.10 (not ^0.15.x), (b) Add a startup assertion that checks the imported function exists and has the expected arity, (c) Add a comment in the upgrade runbook.

title: Error redirect leaks redirect_uri to unauthenticated callers via server_error response
description: In the catch block of /oauth/epds-setup (lines 293-316), on any error the code calls provider.requestManager.get(requestUri) WITHOUT passing deviceId, then redirects to the client's redirect_uri with error=server_error. This means: (1) Any caller who knows a valid request_uri (which is not secret — it's passed in the URL) can trigger an error (e.g., by sending an invalid HMAC) and observe the redirect_uri associated with that request_uri. This is an information leak about which client initiated the request. (2) The error redirect is sent to the client's redirect_uri with error=server_error and iss=pdsUrl. If the redirect_uri is attacker-controlled (e.g., a malicious client registered a redirect_uri), this is fine. But if the error occurs before HMAC verification (it doesn't — HMAC is checked first), this could be exploited. In the current code, HMAC verification happens before the try block, so the error redirect only fires for authenticated requests. However, the requestManager.get() in the catch block does not pass deviceId, which means it may return data for a request that belongs to a different device — a minor inconsistency. The same pattern exists in epds-callback, so this is not a regression.

title: Fix: rewrite /oauth/epds-setup to redirect to /oauth/authorize instead of rendering page directly
close_reason: 773ab93 fix: rewrite /oauth/epds-setup to redirect to /oauth/authorize

title: Simplify complete.ts — remove consent branching, always redirect to /oauth/epds-setup
close_reason: 0be3125 simplify complete.ts: remove consent branching, always redirect to /oauth/epds-setup

title: Fix: Sec-Fetch-Site same-site rejected by stock /oauth/authorize — use client-side redirect to break chain
description:
## Files - packages/pds-core/src/index.ts (modify) ## Context When /oauth/epds-setup redirects 303 to /oauth/authorize, the browser preserves the Sec-Fetch-Site header from the original navigation chain (which started from auth.epds.zimchu.com). Since auth-service and pds-core are on different subdomains (same-site but different origins), the browser reports Sec-Fetch-Site: same-site. The stock @atproto/oauth-provider middleware only allows same-origin, cross-site, or none — it rejects same-site with "Forbidden sec-fetch-site header". ## What to do In the /oauth/epds-setup route handler, replace the 303 redirect with a client-side redirect that breaks the navigation chain. Instead of: ```ts res.redirect(303, authorizeUrl.toString()) ``` Use: ```ts // Use a client-side redirect to break the Sec-Fetch-Site chain. // A 303 redirect preserves the original initiator (auth-service subdomain), // causing the browser to report Sec-Fetch-Site: same-site, which the stock // @atproto/oauth-provider middleware rejects. A client-side redirect starts // a fresh navigation with Sec-Fetch-Site: none. const escapedUrl = authorizeUrl.toString().replace(/"/g, """) res.setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html; charset=utf-8") res.setHeader("Cache-Control", "no-store") res.send(`

Redirecting to authorization...

`) ``` This renders a tiny HTML page that immediately navigates to /oauth/authorize via JavaScript (with meta refresh as fallback). The browser treats this as a new user-initiated navigation, so Sec-Fetch-Site becomes "none" which is in the allowed list. ### Security considerations - The URL is constructed from pdsUrl (trusted) + request_uri + client_id (from the verified HMAC payload) - HTML-escape the URL in the meta tag to prevent injection - JS-escape the URL in the script tag to prevent injection - The page has Cache-Control: no-store to prevent caching ## Dont - Do NOT modify the Sec-Fetch-Site header with middleware (fragile, could mask real attacks) - Do NOT modify any @atproto/oauth-provider code - Do NOT change any other part of the route handler
close_reason: d5d40d8 fix: use client-side redirect in /oauth/epds-setup to avoid Sec-Fetch-Site rejection

title: Fix: inject login_hint into stored PAR parameters so stock consent page skips account picker
close_reason: 74a97f3 inject login_hint into stored PAR params to skip account picker


Keep all other imports and router mounts unchanged.

## Dont

title: Update tests for consent page removal and epds-setup route
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/__tests__/*.test.ts (modify — any tests referencing consent.ts or /auth/consent)
- packages/pds-core/src/__tests__/*.test.ts (modify — if any tests reference /oauth/epds-callback)

## What to do

Search for and update any existing tests that reference the removed consent page or the old callback endpoint.

### 1. Find affected tests
Run: `grep -r "consent" packages/auth-service/src/__tests__/ packages/pds-core/src/__tests__/ 2>/dev/null`
Run: `grep -r "epds-callback" packages/auth-service/src/__tests__/ packages/pds-core/src/__tests__/ 2>/dev/null`
Run: `grep -r "hasClientLogin\|recordClientLogin\|getDidByEmail" packages/auth-service/src/__tests__/ 2>/dev/null`

### 2. For each affected test file:
- If the test imports from consent.ts → remove the import and any tests that exercise the consent route
- If the test mocks hasClientLogin or recordClientLogin in the context of complete.ts → remove those mocks and assertions
- If the test asserts that complete.ts redirects to /auth/consent → update to assert redirect to /oauth/epds-setup
- If the test asserts that complete.ts calls getDidByEmail → remove that assertion
- If the test references /oauth/epds-callback → update to /oauth/epds-setup where appropriate (but note: epds-callback still exists, so only update tests that are specifically testing the new flow)

### 3. Verify
Run `pnpm test` and ensure all tests pass.

## Dont
- Do NOT write new tests for the /oauth/epds-setup route itself (that requires a running PDS instance and is out of scope for unit tests).
- Do NOT delete test files entirely unless they ONLY test consent.ts functionality.
- Do NOT modify tests unrelated to this epic.
close_reason: d09d68b update consent.test.ts: remove consent route references, update to reflect complete.ts/epds-setup flow

title: epds-setup: account variable may be undefined after failed retry loop
description: In /oauth/epds-setup (pds-core/src/index.ts ~line 164-207), 'account' is declared as 'let account: any' and only assigned inside the retry for-loop. If all 3 attempts throw (attempt === 2 re-throws), the catch propagates correctly. However the pattern is not statically provable — if the loop exits without assigning (impossible today but fragile), account.sub on line 207 would throw a runtime TypeError. The fix is to add an explicit guard after the else-branch: 'if (!account) throw new Error("account creation failed")' to make the invariant clear and prevent silent undefined.sub access.

title: pds-core/index.ts: duplicate redirect-URL-building logic between epds-setup and epds-callback
description: The auto-approve redirect branch in /oauth/epds-setup (lines 243-269) duplicates ~30 lines of redirect URL construction that already exist verbatim in /oauth/epds-callback (lines 484-506). Both blocks build a redirect URL with iss/code/state params, handle fragment vs query response_mode, set Cache-Control: no-store, and call res.redirect(303). Any future fix (e.g. adding nonce, changing response_mode handling) must be applied in two places. Extract into a shared helper function, e.g. 'function buildAuthCodeRedirect(redirectUri, code, state, responseMode, pdsUrl): string' in a local lib file or inline helper within index.ts.

title: pds-core/index.ts: @ts-ignore should be @ts-expect-error for sendAuthorizePageFactory import
description: Line 31 of pds-core/src/index.ts uses '// @ts-ignore' to suppress the missing type declarations for the internal @atproto/oauth-provider import. TypeScript best practice (and the stricter lint rule @typescript-eslint/ban-ts-comment) prefers '@ts-expect-error' because it fails the build if the error is ever resolved (i.e. if @atproto/oauth-provider starts exporting types for this path), preventing stale suppressions. The existing eslint-disable comment on line 30 suppresses ban-ts-comment, which means the weaker @ts-ignore is silently accepted. Change to @ts-expect-error and update the eslint-disable comment accordingly.

title: pds-core/index.ts: file-level JSDoc and section header still describe old epds-callback flow
description: The file-level JSDoc (lines 1-15) still describes the old flow ending at step 4 'Auth service redirects to pds.example/oauth/epds-callback' and step 5 'ePDS callback: creates account if needed, issues code, redirects to client'. The new primary flow goes through /oauth/epds-setup which renders the stock consent page; epds-callback is now a secondary/legacy path. Additionally the section header comment at lines 57-62 says 'Called by the auth service after OTP verification + user consent' which is now inaccurate (consent happens in epds-setup, not before epds-callback). Update the file-level JSDoc to reflect the new two-route architecture and update the epds-callback section header to note it is the legacy direct-issue path.

title: complete.ts and crypto.ts: new_account field is vestigial dead weight in the HMAC payload
description: complete.ts now hardcodes new_account: '0' (line 93) and epds-setup ignores the value entirely — it determines new vs existing account itself by querying the PDS accountManager. The new_account field is still included in the HMAC payload (CallbackParams interface in shared/src/crypto.ts) purely for signature compatibility with epds-callback. This creates a misleading API: callers must pass a value that has no semantic meaning for epds-setup. Options: (a) add a comment in complete.ts explaining the field is included only for HMAC compat with epds-callback, (b) longer term, create a separate CallbackParams type for epds-setup that omits new_account, or (c) remove new_account from the HMAC payload entirely once epds-callback is retired. At minimum, add a comment on line 93 of complete.ts explaining why new_account is hardcoded to '0'.

title: Fix: success admonition color fails WCAG AA contrast in light mode (from ePDS-aq2.1)
description:
Review of ePDS-aq2.1 found: --color-success: rgb(23 204 136) renders at 1.95:1 contrast ratio against the admonition background (rgba(23,204,136,0.08) on white ≈ rgb(236,250,245)) in light mode. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text (14px). The 'Code resent!' success message is the only user-visible text using this color.

Evidence:
- .admonition.success { color: var(--color-success); background: rgba(23, 204, 136, 0.08); }
- rgb(23,204,136) on rgb(236,250,245) = 1.95:1 contrast ratio
- WCAG AA minimum for text < 18px = 4.5:1
- Dark mode is fine: 8.41:1 on dark admonition background

Fix: darken --color-success for light mode, e.g. use a darker green like rgb(15,140,93) which achieves ~4.6:1 on white, or add a @media (prefers-color-scheme: light) override for --color-success in the admonition context.

title: Fix: primary email displayed unmasked on /account but step asserts masked pattern (from ePDS-geiz)
description:
Review of ePDS-geiz found: account-settings.ts renderSettingsPage() renders the primary email unmasked:
Primary Email: ${escapeHtml(opts.email)}
But account.steps.ts line 183-185 asserts it is masked: const bodyText = await this.page.locator('body').innerText() expect(bodyText).toMatch(/[a-z0-9*]+\*+@[a-z0-9.-]+/) This regex requires at least one asterisk before the @, which will never match the unmasked email (e.g. 'user-1234@example.com'). The 'the page displays their primary email (masked)' step will always fail. Two possible fixes: 1. Update the step to assert the email is present (unmasked) — change the assertion to match the actual behavior. 2. Update account-settings.ts to mask the email using maskEmail() from @certified-app/shared before rendering. The plan (docs/design/e2e-step-definitions-plan.md) does not specify which is intended. The Gherkin says '(masked)' so option 2 is likely the intended behavior. File this for the Lead to decide.
close_reason: d739cec fix: mask primary email on /account settings page

title: Eliminate auth server redirect in flow 1 (app has own email form)
description:
In flow 1, where the client app collects the user's email itself and passes it as login_hint, there is no reason to redirect through /oauth/authorize at all before showing the OTP input. The current flow is:

1. App collects email
2. App POSTs to /api/oauth/login → PAR → redirects to /oauth/authorize?login_hint=email
3. Auth server renders login page, JS auto-sends OTP, transitions to OTP step
4. User enters OTP

Step 3 is unnecessary indirection. Ideally the app could redirect directly to an OTP input page on the auth server, skipping the intermediate login page entirely. This would require a dedicated endpoint (e.g. /oauth/authorize/otp-challenge) that accepts login_hint, sends OTP server-side, and renders the OTP input directly — no login form involved at all.
notes: Even if the redirect could be eliminated for OTP-based login, it would need to be retained for other authentication mechanisms (e.g. passkeys). The AT Protocol OAuth spec requires the PAR + redirect to /oauth/authorize flow. So any work here should not remove the redirect as a code path — at most it could add a shortcut for OTP specifically while keeping the general redirect path available. Consider whether the complexity is worth it given passkeys will need the redirect anyway.
close_reason: Won't fix. The redirect through /oauth/authorize is mandated by the AT Protocol OAuth spec (PAR → request_uri → /oauth/authorize), not optional indirection — it cannot be skipped without breaking the protocol. Additionally, the redirect is the correct place for credential selection: assuming email OTP before consulting the user's registered credentials would need to be unwound when passkeys or social-only accounts are added. Both points are documented in docs/design/otp-send-client-vs-server.md.

title: Investigate server-side OTP send on login_hint (magic-pds vs current approach)
description:
The old magic-pds branch handled login_hint by sending the OTP server-side in the GET /oauth/authorize handler and rendering the OTP form directly as a full-page response (no flash, no JS transition needed). See authorize.ts on the magic-pds branch.

The current main branch unified everything into a single page (login-page.ts) with CSS show/hide and a JS-driven OTP send on page load. The uncommitted changes in login-page.ts are a partial improvement (pre-render OTP step server-side) but still send the OTP client-side via JS, so the subtitle 'We sent a code to...' is inaccurate at render time.

The proper fix is likely to restore the server-side OTP send approach from magic-pds, but the unified single-page architecture is different enough that this needs careful thought.

Investigation needed (requires owner input):

1. Produce Mermaid sequence diagrams of both approaches (blocked on ePDS-n83 docs restructure, then ePDS-y1l)
2. Review the diagrams with the owner to decide which approach to adopt
3. Decide what to do with the current uncommitted changes in login-page.ts (revert, keep, or replace)
4. Implement the agreed approach

Key constraint: the unified single-page architecture may make a pure server-side send harder (better-auth OTP send is async and involves HTTP calls to the better-auth backend), so tradeoffs need discussion.
close_reason: Investigation complete. The Method-Aware Hybrid approach was chosen (design doc updated by PR #1) and implemented in atproto-ke8. Server-side GET sends no OTP; client-side POST fires after pre-rendered OTP form. Subtitle inaccuracy fixed (shows 'Sending...' until POST succeeds).

title: Fix: nested <form> in renderOtpForm() in recovery.ts is invalid HTML (from pre-deploy review)
description:
## Problem Pre-deployment review found that `renderOtpForm()` in `packages/auth-service/src/routes/recovery.ts` contains a nested `
` element (line 384) inside the outer `` (line 359). ```html ...
``` HTML spec forbids nested forms. Browsers handle this via error recovery (implicitly closing the outer form), but behavior is technically undefined. ## Functional impact In practice, browsers (Chrome/Firefox/Safari) handle this gracefully: - The 'Sign In with Certified' button (before the inner form tag) submits the outer form correctly - The 'Resend Code' button submits the inner form correctly - The `display:contents` style does not fix the nesting issue ## Fix Replace the inner `
` with a `
close_reason: 0253522 fix: unnest resend form in renderOtpForm() using form= attribute

title: Fix: async handler without await in POST /account/backup-email/remove (from ePDS-01p.3)
description: Review of ePDS-01p.3 found: The POST /account/backup-email/remove handler (account-settings.ts line 226) is declared 'async' but contains no 'await' expression after the refactor. Before the refactor, it called 'await getDidByEmail(...)'; now it reads 'res.locals.did' synchronously. This causes a @typescript-eslint/require-await lint error. Evidence: 'pnpm lint' reports: account-settings.ts:226:41 error Async arrow function has no 'await' expression. Fix: remove the 'async' keyword from the handler arrow function on line 226.

title: Add a demo template/design system to epds-demo
description: The epds-demo package currently uses all inline styles with no template, CSS framework, or design system. Add a proper demo template (e.g. Tailwind, shadcn, or a lightweight CSS approach) to make the demo app look polished and be easier to extend with new pages/features.

title: Fix: AUTH_SERVICE_URL env var undocumented in .env.example (from ePDS-1k2.6)
description:
Review of ePDS-1k2.6 found: The new `AUTH_SERVICE_URL` environment variable used in packages/pds-core/src/index.ts (line 200-201) for the handle collision redirect is not documented in .env.example or any per-package .env.example file.

Evidence:
- packages/pds-core/src/index.ts line 200: `const authServiceUrl = process.env.AUTH_SERVICE_URL || 'http://auth:3001'`
- .env.example: no mention of AUTH_SERVICE_URL
- The existing AUTH_HOSTNAME variable (line 35) is also not in .env.example but is used for the same purpose (browser-facing auth redirects)

Note: This issue is secondary to the critical bug (ePDS-01w) where AUTH_SERVICE_URL should be replaced with AUTH_HOSTNAME entirely. If that fix is applied, this issue becomes moot. File this as a reminder to document AUTH_HOSTNAME in .env.example for pds-core operators who need to configure it.

title: handleMode not validated at type level: AuthFlowRow.handleMode is string|null, not HandleMode|null
description: Review of 0962a39 found: The AuthFlowRow interface in db.ts (line 39) types handleMode as 'string | null', not as the HandleMode union type ('random' | 'picker' | 'picker-with-random' | null). This means complete.ts compares flow.handleMode === 'random' against an untyped string, and TypeScript cannot catch a typo like 'Random' or 'RANDOM'. The HandleMode type is currently defined inside the login-page.ts handler scope (not exported), so it cannot be reused in db.ts or complete.ts. The fix is to: (1) export HandleMode from a shared location (e.g. @certified-app/shared types or a dedicated types.ts in auth-service), (2) type AuthFlowRow.handleMode as HandleMode | null, (3) move VALID_HANDLE_MODES to module scope. This would give compile-time safety on all handleMode comparisons. Evidence: packages/shared/src/db.ts line 39; packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts lines 50-61; packages/auth-service/src/routes/complete.ts line 106.

title: Fix: dead .inline-form CSS rule in recovery.ts (from ePDS-8u7.1 + ePDS-8u7.2)
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/recovery.ts (modify)

## What to do
Delete the dead `.inline-form` CSS rule from the `getBaseCSS()` function. It was added by ePDS-8u7.1 to replace an inline `style="display:inline;"` on a nested `<form>`, but ePDS-8u7.2 then removed that nested form entirely. The CSS rule is now dead code — no element in recovery.ts uses `class="inline-form"`.

Find the line in `getBaseCSS()` that reads:
```css

```
Delete that line (and any associated comment if present).

## Don't

- Don't change any other CSS rules
- Don't modify any other file

title: Fix: overflow:hidden on .input-container clips focus ring (from ePDS-aq2.1)
description:
Review of ePDS-aq2.1 found: The .input-container CSS rule has `overflow: hidden` (line 466) which clips the `box-shadow` focus ring defined in .input-container:focus-within (line 477).

Evidence:
- Line 466: `overflow: hidden;` on .input-container
- Line 477: `box-shadow: 0 0 0 2px var(--color-primary), 0 0 0 3px var(--color-contrast-0);` on :focus-within
- box-shadow is not clipped by overflow:hidden in most browsers, BUT the outer ring (3px) may be partially clipped depending on browser rendering. More critically, the .btn-primary also has overflow:hidden (line 600) which clips its focus-visible outline.

The .btn-primary:focus-visible rule (line 609) sets `outline: 2px solid var(--color-contrast-1000); outline-offset: 2px;` but the `overflow: hidden` on .btn-primary (line 600) will clip this outline in some browsers.

Fix: Remove `overflow: hidden` from .input-container and .btn-primary. The border-radius clipping can be achieved with `border-radius` alone on child elements, or use `clip-path` only when needed. The focus ring must be visible for WCAG 2.4.7 compliance.

title: Security: deleteUser on drift does not delete backup_email rows — orphaned rows keyed by DID remain queryable
description: Review of ePDS-01p.3 found: when email drift is detected and deleteUser is called, the spec comment says 'backup_email rows survive deleteUser — this is correct because the PDS account still exists, only the email changed. Backup emails are keyed by DID, not better-auth user ID.' This reasoning is partially correct but has a security implication that was not fully analysed. After drift: (1) The PDS account still exists with the new email. (2) The better-auth user row for the OLD email is deleted. (3) backup_email rows for the DID remain in epds.sqlite. (4) The user logs back in with their new email — a NEW better-auth user row is created. (5) The new user row has a different better-auth user.id. (6) The /account page calls ctx.db.getBackupEmails(did) using res.locals.did — this correctly retrieves the old backup emails by DID, so they are still visible. This part is fine. The concern: if an attacker registers a new better-auth account with the OLD email (after the drift deleted the original row), they get a new better-auth user.id. They cannot log in via OTP because the PDS no longer has that email. But if a social provider (Google/GitHub) is configured and the attacker controls a Google account with the old email, they could create a new better-auth row via social login. This new row would have a different DID (from getDidByEmail returning null for the old email, so isNewAccount=true in complete.ts). The backup_email rows are keyed by the ORIGINAL DID, not the new attacker DID, so the attacker cannot access them. This specific path is safe. However, the broader issue is that backup_email rows for a DID are never cleaned up when the better-auth user is deleted via drift. If the PDS account is later fully deleted (via /account/delete), ctx.db.deleteAccountData(did) IS called — but only from the /account/delete handler, which requires an active better-auth session. After drift-triggered deleteUser, there is no code path that calls deleteAccountData(did). The backup_email rows become permanently orphaned. Fix: in the drift handling block in requireBetterAuth, after deleteUser succeeds, also call ctx.db.deleteAccountData(res.locals.did) — but note that res.locals.did is not set at that point in the middleware (it is set only on the success path). The DID must be retrieved from the getDidByEmail result before the drift branch. Wait — in the drift case result.did === null, so the DID is not available. This means there is no way to clean up backup_email rows on drift without an additional PDS lookup by the old email before the email changes. This is a design gap that needs a separate solution. Evidence: packages/auth-service/src/routes/account-settings.ts:54-76, packages/shared/src/db.ts (deleteAccountData).

title: Fix: recovery .field CSS adds margin-bottom:16px but login-page .field does not — divergence (from ePDS-aq2.5)
Security: no test coverage for requireBetterAuth drift detection — the core security mechanism is untested
description: Review of ePDS-01p.3 found: the drift detection logic in requireBetterAuth (account-settings.ts lines 37-76) has zero test coverage. The acceptance criteria for ePDS-01p.3 only required 'pnpm test passes' — it did not require new tests for the drift detection itself. The three critical branches are untested: (1) getDidByEmail returns null (PDS error) → should return 503 without touching session. (2) getDidByEmail returns { did: null } (drift) → should call deleteUser, call signOut, redirect to /account/login. (3) getDidByEmail returns { did: string } (normal) → should set res.locals.did and call next(). The deleteUser and signOut calls in the drift branch are particularly important to test because: the signOut cookie-clearing bug (ePDS-0e28) would have been caught by a test that asserts Set-Cookie is present on the response. The double-delete race condition (two concurrent requests both seeing drift) would be caught by a test that mocks deleteUser to throw on second call. The 503 path (PDS unavailable) is the safety valve that prevents false-positive account deletion — it must be tested. Fix: add unit tests for requireBetterAuth in a new test file packages/auth-service/src/__tests__/account-settings.test.ts using mock req/res objects (same pattern as csrf.test.ts and rate-limit.test.ts). Mock auth.api.getSession, auth..internalAdapter.deleteUser, auth.api.signOut, and getDidByEmail. Test all three branches. Evidence: packages/auth-service/src/routes/account-settings.ts:22-86, no corresponding test file exists.

title: Fix: new accounts (picker and random mode) never get recordClientLogin called — consent screen shown on every second login
description:
Review of the handle mode system found: after account creation via picker mode (choose-handle.ts POST) or random mode (complete.ts step 5a), recordClientLogin() is never called for the new account.

On the second login for the same email+client combination, complete.ts evaluates:
  needsConsent = !isNewAccount && clientId && !ctx.db.hasClientLogin(email, clientId)
Since hasClientLogin returns false (never recorded), needsConsent=true and the consent screen is shown unexpectedly.

Affected paths:
- Picker mode: choose-handle.ts POST redirects to epds-callback without calling recordClientLogin. The auth_flow is intentionally kept alive for handle_taken retries, so complete.ts step 5d (which calls recordClientLogin) is never reached for new accounts.
- Random mode: complete.ts step 5a redirects to epds-callback without calling recordClientLogin.

The fix: call ctx.db.recordClientLogin(email, clientId) before the redirect in both paths:
1. In complete.ts step 5a (random mode), add recordClientLogin before the redirect.
2. In choose-handle.ts POST, add recordClientLogin before the redirect (requires passing clientId from flow).

Evidence: packages/auth-service/src/routes/complete.ts lines 105-139 (random mode, no recordClientLogin); packages/auth-service/src/routes/choose-handle.ts lines 320-346 (POST success path, no recordClientLogin); packages/auth-service/src/routes/complete.ts lines 102-103 (needsConsent logic).

title: Fix: recovery link passes pdsPublicUrl instead of real request_uri (from ePDS-aq2.1)
description:
Review of ePDS-aq2.1 (da62854) found: the recovery link in renderLoginPage uses opts.pdsPublicUrl as the request_uri parameter instead of the actual PAR request_uri.

Evidence (login-page.ts line 827):
  <a href="/auth/recover?request_uri=${encodeURIComponent(opts.pdsPublicUrl)}">

The recovery route (recovery.ts) uses this request_uri to:
1. Create a new auth_flow row if the cookie is missing (line 88) — with wrong requestUri
2. Render a 'Back to sign in' link: /oauth/authorize?request_uri=<value> — this will be the PDS base URL, not a valid PAR request_uri, so clicking 'Back to sign in' from the recovery page will fail with 'Missing request_uri' or show a broken flow

The ui-design branch (the spec reference) correctly adds requestUri to the renderLoginPage signature and passes opts.requestUri to the recovery link. The spec said 'no new parameters' but explicitly listed only defaultBrandColor/defaultBgColor/defaultPanelColor — requestUri was omitted from that exclusion list.

Fix: add requestUri to renderLoginPage opts (matching ui-design branch), pass ctx.config.pdsPublicUrl's actual request_uri from the route handler, and use opts.requestUri in the recovery link href.

title: Epic: Restyle auth login page to match atproto OAuth UI
description: Restyle the ePDS OAuth login page (login-page.ts) to visually match the atproto oauth-provider-ui design language. The current page uses ad-hoc inline CSS with a warm beige background, basic bordered inputs, and no dark mode. The target is the atproto split-panel layout with pill-shaped inputs, SVG icons, CSS custom property color tokens, and dark mode support. Scope is limited to login-page.ts only (email entry + OTP entry steps). No React migration, no Tailwind, no i18n. Success looks like: a user familiar with bsky.app's OAuth flow would recognize the ePDS login page as belonging to the same design family.

title: Restyle renderLoginPage() to match atproto OAuth UI design
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts (modify) — rewrite the CSS and HTML inside renderLoginPage(), keep all JS logic unchanged

## Reference
The target design is the atproto oauth-provider-ui package at:
  /home/kzoeps/Projects/atproto/packages/oauth/oauth-provider-ui/
Key reference files:
  - src/style.css — CSS custom property color token system
  - src/components/layouts/layout-title-page.tsx — split-panel layout
  - src/components/forms/input-container.tsx — pill input styling
  - src/components/forms/button.tsx — button styling
  - src/components/forms/form-card.tsx — form layout
  - src/components/utils/icons.tsx — SVG icon paths (AtSymbolIcon, TokenIcon, EmailIcon)

## What to do

### 1. CSS Custom Properties (color tokens)
Replace the current ad-hoc colors with a CSS custom property system matching atproto's approach:
- \`--color-primary\`: derived from the branding \`brand_color\` param (default: \`#8338ec\` / rgb(131 56 236) — atproto's purple)
- \`--color-primary-contrast\`: white by default
- A neutral contrast scale (--color-contrast-0 through --color-contrast-1000) using HSL with 20% saturation, keyed to the primary hue
- Semantic tokens: \`--color-text-default\`, \`--color-text-light\`, \`--color-border-default\`
- Dark mode: \`@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)\` block that inverts the contrast scale (contrast-0 becomes darkest, contrast-1000 becomes lightest)
- The existing \`brandColor\` and \`bgColor\` from client branding should override \`--color-primary\` and body background respectively via inline style on \`<html>\` or \`:root\`

### 2. Layout — Split Panel
Replace the current centered-box layout with atproto's split-panel:
- On desktop (min-width: 768px): left panel (50% width) with background \`--color-contrast-25\`, right panel with the form content. Left panel contains the logo, "Sign in" title (styled with \`--color-primary\`), and subtitle. Right panel has the form, max-width ~600px, padded.
- On mobile: stacked vertically. Left panel becomes a top bar with logo + title in a row, then form content below.
- Left panel desktop: right-aligned text, subtle right border in dark mode (\`--color-contrast-200\`)
- Body background: white in light mode, \`--color-contrast-0\` (near-black) in dark mode

### 3. Input Styling — Pill Inputs with Icons
Replace bordered inputs with atproto's pill-input pattern:
- Container: \`min-height: 48px; border-radius: 8px; background: var(--color-contrast-25); overflow: hidden;\` (no border)
- Dark mode container: \`background: var(--color-contrast-50)\`
- Inner layout: flexbox row with icon (24px, centered, \`--color-text-light\`), input field, optional append
- Icon turns \`--color-primary\` on focus (use \`:focus-within\` on the container)
- Focus ring on container: \`box-shadow: 0 0 0 2px var(--color-primary);\` with 1px offset (use outline or box-shadow)
- Input itself: \`background: transparent; border: none; outline: none; width: 100%; font-size: 16px; color: var(--color-text-default);\`
- Transition: \`transition: all 0.3s ease-in-out\` on the container

### 4. Email Input
- Icon: AtSymbolIcon SVG (path: \`M12 4a8 8 0 1 0 4.21 14.804....\` — copy the full path from the reference icons.tsx, the AtSymbolIcon export). Render as inline \`<svg viewBox="0 0 24 24" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor">\`
- Input: \`type="email"\`, same attributes as current
- Label: above the input container as a \`<label>\` with \`font-size: 14px; font-weight: 500; color: var(--color-text-light); margin-bottom: 4px;\`

### 5. OTP Input
- Icon: TokenIcon SVG (path: \`M4 5.5a.5.5 0 0 0-.5.5v2.535...\` — copy from reference). Same inline SVG pattern.
- Keep the monospace, large-letter-spacing, centered-text styling for the OTP code input, but wrap it in the pill container with the icon
- The OTP input should still have: \`font-family: 'SF Mono', Menlo, Consolas, monospace; font-size: 28px; letter-spacing: 8px; text-align: center;\`

### 6. Button Styling
- Primary button: \`background: var(--color-primary); color: var(--color-primary-contrast); border: none; border-radius: 6px; padding: 8px 24px; font-size: 16px; font-weight: 500; cursor: pointer; transition: all 0.3s ease-in-out;\`
- Focus ring: \`outline: 2px solid var(--color-primary); outline-offset: 2px;\`
- Disabled: \`opacity: 0.5\`
- Secondary/text buttons (Resend, Back): \`background: var(--color-contrast-25); color: var(--color-text-light); border: none; border-radius: 6px; padding: 8px 24px;\` Dark mode: \`background: var(--color-contrast-50)\`
- Button row: flex row-reverse with space-between (primary action on the right, cancel/back on the left), matching atproto's \`flex-row-reverse\` pattern

### 7. Social Login Buttons
- Restyle to match: pill-shaped (\`border-radius: 8px\`), \`background: var(--color-contrast-25)\`, icon + text, hover darkens slightly
- Divider: use \`--color-border-default\` for the line color, \`--color-text-light\` for text

### 8. Error Display
- Match atproto's Admonition pattern: \`border-radius: 8px; padding: 12px; border: 1px solid var(--color-border-default); display: flex; gap: 8px;\` with an alert icon SVG on the left
- Error text color: use a red/error semantic color (define \`--color-error: rgb(255 0 110)\`)
- Success messages (code resent): use \`--color-success: rgb(23 204 136)\`

### 9. Recovery Link
- Style as subtle text link: \`color: var(--color-text-light); font-size: 13px; text-decoration: none;\` with hover underline

### 10. Dark Mode
- All of the above must work in dark mode via \`@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)\`
- Body: dark background
- Inputs: slightly lighter than body
- Text: light colors
- Left panel: darker shade with right border

## Don't
- Do NOT change any JavaScript logic (sendOtp, verifyOtp, step transitions, event listeners, auto-send on login_hint)
- Do NOT change the form field names, IDs, or data flow
- Do NOT add any external dependencies (no Tailwind, no CSS framework, no npm packages)
- Do NOT change the route handler logic (flow creation, cookie setting, client metadata resolution)
- Do NOT modify any other files besides login-page.ts
- Do NOT add i18n/localization
- Do NOT use \`import\` for CSS — everything stays as inline \`<style>\` in the template literal (this is server-rendered HTML)
- Do NOT change the renderError() function at the bottom of the file
close_reason: f49b056 restyle renderLoginPage() to match atproto OAuth UI design

title: Add PDS-level branding env vars for auth login page
description: ## Files - packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts (modify) - packages/auth-service/src/context.ts (modify) — add new config fields - packages/auth-service/src/index.ts (modify) — read env vars into config - packages/auth-service/.env.example (modify) — document new vars ## What to do Add 4 new env vars for PDS-level default branding on the auth login page: \`\`\` AUTH_LOGO_URL=https://gainforest.earth/_assets/media/7155311d31a04b4cf1242b10b11062e6.png AUTH_BRAND_COLOR=#3E7053 AUTH_BACKGROUND_COLOR=#F2ECE2 AUTH_PANEL_COLOR=#3E7053 \`\`\` ### 1. Context / config changes In \`src/context.ts\`, add these optional fields to the config type: \`\`\`ts logoUrl?: string brandColor?: string backgroundColor?: string panelColor?: string \`\`\` In \`src/index.ts\` \`main()\`, read from env: \`\`\`ts logoUrl: process.env.AUTH_LOGO_URL || undefined, brandColor: process.env.AUTH_BRAND_COLOR || undefined, backgroundColor: process.env.AUTH_BACKGROUND_COLOR || undefined, panelColor: process.env.AUTH_PANEL_COLOR || undefined, \`\`\` ### 2. Login page changes In \`login-page.ts\`, the \`createLoginPageRouter\` function already has access to \`ctx\`. Pass the config branding into \`renderLoginPage()\` as new options: \`\`\`ts defaultLogoUrl: ctx.config.logoUrl, defaultBrandColor: ctx.config.brandColor, defaultBgColor: ctx.config.backgroundColor, defaultPanelColor: ctx.config.panelColor, \`\`\` Then in \`renderLoginPage()\`, change the priority chain: \`\`\`ts // Priority: client metadata > env var defaults > hardcoded fallbacks const brandColor = b.brand_color || opts.defaultBrandColor || '#8338ec' const bgColor = b.background_color || opts.defaultBgColor || null const panelColor = opts.defaultPanelColor || null // no client metadata equivalent const logoUrl = b.logo_uri || opts.defaultLogoUrl || null \`\`\` For the logo HTML: \`\`\`ts const logoHtml = logoUrl ? \`\` : '' \`\`\` ### 3. Panel color CSS The left panel (\`.title-panel\`) currently uses \`background: var(--color-contrast-25)\` on desktop. When \`panelColor\` is set, override this with the solid color. Add a new CSS custom property \`--color-panel\` that defaults to \`var(--color-contrast-25)\` but can be overridden: - In the \`:root\` block: \`--color-panel: var(--color-contrast-25);\` - In the \`rootStyle\` inline override: if panelColor is set, add \`--color-panel: ${panelColor};\` - The \`.title-panel\` desktop rule uses \`background: var(--color-panel);\` When the panel has a dark background color (like #3E7053), the title text and subtitle need to be light. Add a CSS custom property \`--color-panel-text\` that defaults to \`var(--color-primary)\` for the title and \`var(--color-text-light)\` for the subtitle. When panelColor is set, override both to white: - \`--color-panel-text: #fff;\` (for title h1) - \`--color-panel-subtitle: rgba(255,255,255,0.8);\` (for subtitle) Update the CSS: - \`.title-panel h1 { color: var(--color-panel-text, var(--color-primary)); }\` - \`.title-panel .subtitle { color: var(--color-panel-subtitle, var(--color-text-light)); }\` Only set these overrides in the rootStyle when panelColor is provided. To determine if text should be light, check if the panel color is dark. A simple heuristic: parse the hex color, compute relative luminance, if < 0.5 use white text. Or just always use white text when panelColor is explicitly set (since the user chose a custom color, they presumably want contrast). ### 4. Background color The \`bgColor\` (from AUTH_BACKGROUND_COLOR or client metadata) should set the body background. The existing \`bgColorStyle\` mechanism already handles this — it injects a \`\` block. Just make sure the env var default feeds into this. ### 5. .env.example Add to the "Auth-service only" section: \`\`\` # PDS-level branding defaults (overridden by OAuth client metadata if present) # AUTH_LOGO_URL=https://example.com/logo.png # AUTH_BRAND_COLOR=#8338ec # AUTH_BACKGROUND_COLOR=#F2ECE2 # AUTH_PANEL_COLOR=#3E7053 \`\`\` ## Don't - Do NOT change any JavaScript client-side logic (sendOtp, verifyOtp, etc.) - Do NOT change form field names, IDs, or data flow - Do NOT add npm dependencies - Do NOT change the renderError() function - Do NOT break existing client metadata branding — it must still take priority over env vars
close_reason: 9fee49a feat: add AUTH_LOGO_URL, AUTH_BRAND_COLOR, AUTH_BACKGROUND_COLOR, AUTH_PANEL_COLOR env vars

title: Restyle recovery page to match login page design
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/recovery.ts (modify) — rewrite the CSS and HTML in renderRecoveryForm(), renderOtpForm(), and renderError() to match the login page design

## Reference
The login page at packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts has the target design. Copy the same CSS token system, layout, and component styles.

## What to do

The recovery page currently uses its own ad-hoc CSS (old gray/white card design at the bottom of the file in the \`CSS\` constant). Restyle it to match the login page.

### 1. Replace the CSS constant
Replace the entire \`CSS\` constant at the bottom of the file with the same CSS from login-page.ts. Copy the full CSS from login-page.ts including:
- The :root color token system (--hue-primary, --color-primary, contrast scale, etc.)
- Dark mode @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) inversion
- The split-panel layout (.layout, .title-panel, .title-panel-inner, .form-panel)
- Input container styles (.input-container, .input-inner, .input-icon, .input-field, .otp-input)
- Button styles (.btn-primary, .btn-icon, .link-btn)
- Admonition styles (.admonition, .admonition.error)
- Field/label styles (.field-label, .field, .form-group)
- The .otp-actions, .otp-links, .otp-links-dot styles
- The .title-svg, .client-logo styles

Also add the panel color and brand color CSS custom property overrides. Read them from ctx.config just like login-page.ts does. You'll need to pass ctx into the render functions or compute the rootStyle/bgColorStyle strings in the route handler and pass them as opts.

### 2. Restyle renderRecoveryForm() (email entry step)
Use the same split-panel layout:
- Left panel: GainForest logo (/static/gainforest-logo.png), the SVG title (/static/sign-in-with-certified-title.svg), subtitle "Recover your account"
- Right panel: the form with pill-shaped input (email icon + email input), primary button "Send Recovery Code", and a link-btn "Back to Sign In" linking back to /oauth/authorize?request_uri=...
- Error display: use the admonition pattern (same as login page)

### 3. Restyle renderOtpForm() (OTP verification step)
Same split-panel layout:
- Left panel: same as above
- Right panel: subtitle "If a backup email matches, we sent an 8-digit code to <masked>", pill-shaped OTP input (token icon + code input), primary button with certified icon "Sign In with Certified", and link-btns for "Resend Code" (as a form POST) and "Back to Sign In"
- Error display: admonition pattern

### 4. Restyle renderError()
Simple error page with the same CSS tokens and body styling.

### 5. Branding support
Pass ctx.config.brandColor, ctx.config.backgroundColor, ctx.config.panelColor into the render functions and apply them the same way login-page.ts does (rootStyle inline on <html>, bgColorStyle <style> block).

### 6. SVG icons
Use the same SVG icon paths from login-page.ts:
- AtSymbolIcon for the email input
- TokenIcon for the OTP input
- Alert triangle for error admonition
- Certified green icon for the sign-in button (/static/certified-green-signin.svg)

## Don't
- Do NOT change any route handler logic (GET/POST handlers, OTP sending, verification, cookie handling)
- Do NOT change the form field names, hidden inputs, or action URLs
- Do NOT add external dependencies
- Do NOT modify login-page.ts or any other files
close_reason: 99814d9 restyle recovery page to match login page design
